cells: A step-by-step visual reference for scoring stocks using the Hybrid Qualitative Framework
A structured way to score the "non-number" stuff about a stock — management, moats, regulation, growth runways.
Financial ratios tell you WHAT HAPPENED. This framework tells you WHY it happened and WHETHER IT WILL CONTINUE.
Hybrid = Sector-level overlay (Layer 1) + Industry-specific factors (Layer 2), combined into one composite score.
"What’s the weather in this entire sector?"
Composite Score  =  ( Layer 1 Score  ×  0.40 )  +  ( Layer 2 Score  ×  0.60 )
Result: A single score from 1–10 for every industry — combining sector macro + industry-specific quality
SCORE LAYER 1 — SECTOR OVERLAY  (40% of final)
Score ALL 7 factors below for the sector. Same factors, same weights for every sector. Only the SCORE (1–10) changes.
Is the government helping or hurting this sector?
Hostile, tightening rules, frequent adverse changes
Favorable, liberalizing, clear rules, minimal intervention
Is this sector riding a 10-year growth wave or slowly dying?
Sunset industry, shrinking market (coal, print media)
Is it an oligopoly (3 players) or a bloodbath (10,000 players)?
Fragmented, commoditized, no pricing power, easy entry
Some differentiation, regional moats, moderate competition
Oligopoly, strong moats, top 3–5 control >60% share
Are earnings predictable or do they swing wildly every year?
Highly cyclical, >40% swings, no forward visibility
Moderate, 15–30% variance, some order book visibility
Is technology a THREAT (killing the business) or a TAILWIND (driving growth)?
Existential threat (fossil fuel → renewable, retail → e-commerce)
Adapting — can transition (banks adopting UPI, auto → EV)
Sector IS the disruptor (IT, fintech, SaaS, renewable energy)
Does the sector have structural governance/environmental risks?
High emissions, governance concerns, promoter tunnelling
Mixed track record, improving ESG, manageable compliance
Strong governance culture, ESG leadership, attracts FII flows
Can companies in this sector easily raise money & attract institutional investors?
No institutional interest, thin liquidity, no sector ETFs
Moderate FII/DII ownership, adequate liquidity, in broad indices
Deep institutional ownership, dedicated ETFs, easy capital access
💡 EXAMPLE: Same 7 factors, different scores for different sectors
☝️ KEY INSIGHT: Same 7 factors, same weights — but IT scores 8.30 vs Metals 4.75. The sector "weather" is fundamentally different.
Each sector has 6 PRE-ASSIGNED factors from a menu of 15. You DON’T need to pick — it’s already decided. Just score them 1–10.
WHO is running the company and are their interests aligned with yours?
WHAT stops competitors from stealing this company's lunch?
WHERE and HOW does this company sell — and can others replicate it?
WHAT can go WRONG that's beyond the company's control?
HOW BIG can this get and what fuels the growth engine?
🧠 THE SELECTION LOGIC — "Why does each sector pick what it picks?"
The #1 question: "What is the SINGLE BIGGEST thing that makes or breaks companies in this sector?" The answer determines which factors get the highest weight.
If the sector is driven by...
End consumer makes the purchase decision. Brand trust and shop availability = everything.
FMCG, Consumer Durables, Consumer Services, Hotels
Not Supply Chain (don't have factories), not IP (no patents), not Policy (minimal govt involvement)
People ARE the product. Knowledge creates lock-in. Talent shortage = growth constraint.
Not Distribution (B2B, not retail), not Brand (clients care about delivery, not ads)
Regulatory Moat, Mgmt Quality, Skin in the Game
License scarcity is the moat. One bad management decision can destroy the franchise.
Not Export (domestic business), not Supply Chain (no physical raw materials for banks)
Supply Chain, Environmental Risk, Policy Dependency
Raw material access = survival. Environmental costs are real. Government controls pricing/licenses.
Metals, Chemicals, Oil & Gas, Power, Agriculture
Not Brand (steel is steel — nobody cares who made it), not Switching Costs (commodity = easy to switch)
Technology creates moats. Global markets provide growth. Platform effects create scale.
Not Distribution (digital delivery), not Policy Dependency (market-driven sectors)
💡 KEY CONCEPT: Industries within a sector use the SAME factors but get DIFFERENT scores
Example: All IT industries use Talent, Switching Costs, Exports, Scalability, IP, Mgmt — but scores are very different:
☝️ Same sector, same factors — but Cloud & SaaS (8.15) is qualitatively far superior to BPO (4.85). This is exactly why Layer 2 exists!
COMBINE & INTERPRET — GET THE FINAL ANSWER
Composite = (Layer 1 × 40%) + (Layer 2 × 60%). Then look up which band the score falls in.

The Qualitative Composite feeds into the overall stock scoring model alongside the Quantitative Score from sector fundamentals.
FINAL STOCK SCORE  =  ( Quantitative Score  ×  0.60 )  +  ( Qualitative Composite  ×  0.40 )
60% hard numbers (from 70 fundamentals in sector sheets)  +  40% qualitative judgment (from this framework)
The X-Ray — what’s inside the body today
Shows current financial health (P/E, ROCE, margins, growth)

The Climate — is the environment healthy or toxic?

The Lifestyle — does this person exercise, eat well, have good genes?
Shows the specific qualitative strengths/weaknesses of this industry

The Doctor’s Verdict — overall health prognosis
Single 1–10 score combining sector weather + industry-specific quality
Feeds into final stock score at 40% weight
🚀 5-STEP CHEAT SHEET — How to score ANY stock in 15 minutes
Identify the stock’s sector and industry
‘sector names’ sheet → find the sector, then its sector sheet for industry list
‘Qualitative Framework’ sheet, rows 8–15 for factor rubrics
Look up the 6 pre-assigned Layer 2 factors for your sector
‘Qualitative Framework’ sheet, rows 26–41 for factor rubrics
‘Qualitative Framework’ sheet, rows 52–57 for interpretation bands
✅ Total time: ~13 minutes per stock. Score once, review quarterly.
Hybrid approach: Sector-level overlay (Layer 1) + Industry-specific factors (Layer 2). Score each factor 1–10. Weighted scores combine for qualitative composite.
Applied to ALL industries within a sector. Captures macro-level qualitative risks and tailwinds common to the entire sector.
Hostile/tightening regulation, frequent adverse changes, regulatory uncertainty
Stable regulatory framework, neutral policy stance, predictable changes
Favorable/liberalizing, clear framework, self-regulation, minimal intervention

Regulation is the single biggest qualitative driver for Indian sectors — RBI for Financial Services, IRDAI for Insurance, SEBI for Capital Markets, TRAI for Telecom, FSSAI for FMCG. Policy shifts can redefine industry economics overnight. Score reflects current direction of regulation, not just current state.
Declining/sunset industry, shrinking TAM, structural headwinds (e.g., coal, print media)
Secular multi-year growth >12%, clear structural tailwinds (financialization, digitization, urbanization)

Captures whether the sector is riding a long-term structural wave or facing headwinds. India's per-capita consumption gap vs. global averages creates structural runways in insurance, housing, consumer durables, healthcare. Highest weight because structural demand is the most durable qualitative advantage.
Fragmented, commoditized, no pricing power, low barriers to entry, constant margin pressure from new entrants
Moderate concentration, some differentiation possible, regional moats but national competition, brand matters but isn't decisive
Oligopolistic structure, strong brand/IP/network moats, high switching costs, top 3–5 players control >60% market share

Moat durability separates compounders from cyclical traps. Indian sectors vary enormously — Telecom is a 3-player oligopoly (Jio, Airtel, Vi) with massive entry barriers (₹1.5L Cr spectrum), while Textiles is hyper-fragmented with 10,000+ manufacturers and near-zero pricing power. Financial Services has RBI-created regulatory moats (banking license scarcity). IT Services has scale moats (TCS, Infy have 600K+ employees — replicating that delivery engine takes decades). Score the structural competitive dynamics, not just current market share.
Highly cyclical (commodity-linked, monsoon-dependent, capex-cycle driven), earnings swing >40% peak-to-trough, no forward visibility beyond 1 quarter
Moderately cyclical, earnings vary 15–30% through cycle, some contract/order book visibility (6–12 months), mix of recurring and one-time revenue
Defensive/recurring revenue model, earnings variance <15%, multi-year order books or subscription base, high revenue predictability (annuity, SaaS, regulated returns)

Earnings visibility determines valuation multiple sustainability. Indian markets pay 40–80x P/E for predictable compounders (FMCG, IT Services) but only 8–15x for cyclicals (Metals, Chemicals) despite similar ROE. This isn't irrational — predictable earnings allow confident DCF modelling, institutional accumulation, and lower cost of equity. Sectors with annuity-like cash flows (Power PPAs, IT multi-year contracts, Insurance renewal premiums) deserve structural valuation premiums. Cyclical sectors (Metals, Realty, Chemicals) require timing skill and through-cycle valuation frameworks. Score reflects the inherent earnings pattern, not current-year performance.
Business model under existential threat from technology shifts (print media vs. digital, traditional retail vs. e-commerce, fossil fuels vs. renewables), slow to adapt, legacy infrastructure lock-in
Technology is reshaping the value chain but incumbents can adapt (banks adopting UPI, auto OEMs transitioning to EV), moderate capital required to stay relevant, 3–5 year adaptation window
Technology is a tailwind — sector is the disruptor (IT, Digital, Fintech, Renewable Energy), or technology amplifies existing moats (AI in pharma R&D, IoT in manufacturing, precision agriculture)

India is experiencing simultaneous technology disruptions across sectors — UPI is reshaping payments (Financial Services), EV transition threatens ICE auto (Automobiles), GenAI is redefining IT Services delivery models, e-commerce is compressing traditional retail margins, and renewable energy is making thermal power plants stranded assets. Unlike developed markets where disruption plays out over decades, India's leapfrog adoption (500M UPI users in 5 years, 5G rollout in 18 months) compresses disruption timelines. Score reflects whether technology is an existential threat, a manageable transition, or a structural growth driver for the sector. Sectors scoring 1–3 need a 'disruption discount' in valuation; sectors scoring 7–10 deserve a 'digital premium'.

Sector-wide governance concerns (promoter pledge >50%, related-party tunnelling, opaque holding structures), high environmental liability exposure, poor labour practices, frequent SEBI/regulatory actions
Mixed governance track record, improving ESG disclosure, some promoter concentration but adequate board independence, manageable environmental compliance costs

Strong governance culture (independent boards, transparent reporting, minimal pledging), ESG leadership (net-zero commitments, circular economy), attracts FII/DII flows, BRSR compliance ahead of mandate

ESG is no longer optional in Indian markets — SEBI's BRSR (Business Responsibility and Sustainability Reporting) mandate, MSCI/Sustainalytics screening for index inclusion, and FII allocation frameworks increasingly gate capital on ESG scores. Sectors with structural ESG risks (Metals — emissions, mining displacement; Power — coal dependency, ash disposal; Chemicals — effluent, hazmat) face rising compliance costs that permanently compress margins by 2–5%. Governance quality determines whether minority shareholders participate in value creation or watch promoters extract it — Indian markets have a long history of promoter tunnelling (related-party transactions, pledging, preferential allotments at discounts). Score reflects sector-level governance DNA and ESG exposure, not individual company behaviour.
Limited institutional interest, poor index representation, thin liquidity (avg daily volume <₹5 Cr for most stocks), difficulty raising equity/debt capital, no dedicated sector ETFs/MF schemes
Moderate institutional ownership (FII+DII 20–40%), adequate liquidity for mid-caps, sector represented in broad indices but not in top weight, occasional capital raises successful
Deep institutional ownership (FII+DII >40%), dedicated sector indices and ETFs (Nifty Bank, Nifty IT, Nifty Pharma), high liquidity, easy access to equity and debt capital markets, regular FII flows

Capital market access creates a self-reinforcing cycle — sectors with deep liquidity and index representation attract more institutional flows, which improve price discovery, reduce volatility, and lower cost of capital. Nifty Bank and Nifty IT have dedicated ETFs with ₹50,000+ Cr AUM each, ensuring continuous institutional demand. Financial Services commands ~35% weight in Nifty 50, making it virtually impossible for large-cap fund managers to underweight. Conversely, sectors like Textiles, Forest Materials, and Agriculture have minimal institutional interest — most stocks trade <₹10 Cr daily, making position building difficult for MFs. This illiquidity discount is real: two companies with identical fundamentals will trade at different multiples purely based on sector liquidity. Score reflects the structural capital market ecosystem around the sector, not individual stock liquidity.
Result: Sector Qualitative Score (1–10 scale). Apply uniformly to ALL industries within the sector before adding Layer 2 adjustments.
Applied at the INDUSTRY level within each sector. Captures micro-level qualitative nuances that differentiate industries from each other within the same sector.
Unlike Layer 1 (fixed 7 factors for all sectors), Layer 2 uses a MENU of qualitative factors — each industry picks 5–7 factors most relevant to its competitive dynamics.
The factor MENU and scoring rubrics are defined below. Each industry's specific factor selection is noted in its sector sheet.
Management Quality & Capital Allocation Track Record
History of value-destructive M&A, empire-building capex with sub-WACC returns, frequent equity dilution, related-party transactions benefiting promoters, poor communication with investors
Competent operational management, adequate capital allocation (ROCE ~WACC), occasional missteps but self-correcting, reasonable investor communication, no major governance red flags

Exceptional capital allocators (ROCE consistently >2x WACC), disciplined M&A with proven integration, counter-cyclical investment, transparent guidance with high accuracy, founder-led with skin in the game (>50% holding, zero pledge)
All industries — but especially critical for Capital Goods, Diversified Conglomerates, Financial Services, Realty (capital-intensive sectors where management decisions compound over decades)
First-generation promoter with no succession plan, family disputes visible, key-man risk (business = one person), professional management revolving door (3+ CEO changes in 5 years)
Established promoter family with second-generation involvement, professional CEO with 3–5 year tenure, board has adequate independent directors, succession discussed but not formalized

Multi-generational promoter with smooth transitions (Bajaj, Godrej, Tata, Murugappa), or founder-led with clearly groomed successor, deep professional management bench, institutional governance culture

Family-run businesses (FMCG, Consumer Durables, Realty, Textiles, Diversified), Banks (CEO succession is RBI-regulated), Insurance, IT Services (founder transitions: Infy post-Murthy, Wipro post-Premji)
Skin in the Game (Insider Ownership & Alignment)
Promoter holding <25%, high pledge ratio (>40% of promoter shares pledged), recent insider selling, ESOPs at deep discount without performance vesting, misaligned incentive structures
Promoter holding 35–50%, minimal pledge (<10%), occasional ESOP grants with standard vesting, management compensation linked to revenue/profit targets but not TSR

Promoter holding >50% with zero pledge, incremental buying on dips, management ESOPs tied to long-term TSR/ROCE targets, significant personal wealth tied to company stock, aligned long-term incentives

All industries — universally applicable. Especially important for mid-cap and small-cap companies where promoter behaviour is the single biggest risk factor. Track promoter pledge trends over 8 quarters, not just current snapshot.
Commodity product with zero brand premium, price-taker in the market, customers switch on 1–2% price difference, no differentiation beyond specification compliance
Recognized brand within the industry but not top-of-mind, 5–10% pricing premium over generics, moderate customer loyalty, brand matters for new customer acquisition but not retention
Category-defining brand (Asian Paints, Titan, Pidilite, HDFC Bank), 15–30% pricing premium sustained over cycles, customers resist switching even at price disadvantage, brand is the moat itself

FMCG, Consumer Durables, Paints, Jewellery, Retail Banking, Insurance, Hospitals, Diagnostics, Consumer Services — any B2C industry where the end consumer makes the purchase decision. Less relevant for B2B/commodity industries (Metals, Chemicals, Power).
Zero switching costs, customers can shift overnight, no integration or data lock-in, contracts are short-term or non-existent, commoditized service delivery
Moderate switching costs (6–12 month transition), some data/process integration, contractual lock-in for 1–2 years, switching is possible but inconvenient and mildly costly

Very high switching costs — deep system integration (ERP, core banking), multi-year contracts with penalties, regulatory approvals required for switching, mission-critical dependency (cloud infrastructure, payment rails)

IT Services (multi-year engagements, deep integration), Financial Services (core banking switch takes 2–3 years), Cloud & SaaS (data migration costs), Telecom (number portability reduced but still exists), Healthcare (hospital-patient relationships, insurance network tie-ups)
No proprietary technology, relies entirely on licensed/imported IP, zero patents or trade secrets, easy to replicate by any well-capitalized competitor, no R&D culture
Some proprietary processes or formulations, moderate patent portfolio (10–50 active patents), R&D team exists but follows rather than leads, innovation is incremental not breakthrough

Deep proprietary IP (100+ patents, proprietary molecules/processes, trade secrets), R&D creates sustainable competitive advantage, innovation pipeline visible for 5+ years, IP licensing generates royalty income

Pharmaceuticals (ANDA filings, biosimilar development), Specialty Chemicals (proprietary synthesis routes), Software Products (source code, algorithms), Biotechnology, Medical Equipment, Defence & Aerospace, AI & Data Analytics, Cybersecurity
Weak distribution, <1,000 dealers/touchpoints, no direct customer access, dependent on 1–2 intermediaries for >50% of sales, digital channel absent
Moderate distribution (5,000–15,000 touchpoints), mix of direct and indirect channels, growing digital presence (5–15% of revenue online), regional strength but national gaps

Pan-India distribution dominance (>25,000 touchpoints), direct customer relationships, omni-channel presence (offline + online + D2C), distribution built over decades and nearly impossible to replicate

FMCG (Hindustan Unilever's 9M+ outlets), Consumer Durables, Paints (Asian Paints 70,000+ dealer network), Agrochemicals & Fertilizers, Cement, Farm Equipment, Building Materials — any industry where last-mile distribution is the competitive bottleneck
Entirely domestic, no export capability, products don't meet global standards (US FDA, EU CE, ISO), no presence in international markets, currency risk not relevant
Emerging exporter (10–25% of revenue), meets basic international certifications, present in 5–15 countries, competitive on cost but not yet on quality/brand in global markets
Global leader or top-5 exporter from India (>40% export revenue), multi-geography presence (30+ countries), global certifications (US FDA, EU GMP, REACH), CRAMS/CSM contracts with global innovators

Pharmaceuticals (India = pharmacy of the world), IT Services (75%+ exports), Specialty Chemicals (China+1 beneficiary), Agrochemicals, Textiles (garment exports), Auto Components, Gems & Jewellery. Less relevant for domestic-focused sectors (Realty, Power, Financial Services, Telecom).
Low entry barriers, no licensing requirement, anyone with capital can enter, fragmented market with continuous new entrants, government policy encourages competition
Moderate entry barriers — requires specialized skills, moderate capital investment, or technical certifications but not scarce licenses. New entrants appear every 2–3 years.

High regulatory barriers — requires scarce government licenses (banking, insurance, telecom spectrum, mining lease, defence license), 5–10 year approval cycles, limited new licenses issued, incumbents structurally protected

Banking (RBI license — only 2 new universal bank licenses in 10 years), Insurance (IRDAI), Telecom (spectrum auctions), Mining (lease allocation), Defence (DPIIT license), Airports (PPP concessions), Power Distribution (area franchise). The rarest and most valuable moat in Indian markets — government-created scarcity.
Single-source dependency for critical inputs, 80%+ imported raw materials, no backward integration, supply disruptions in last 3 years caused >10% revenue impact
Diversified supplier base (3–5 sources for key inputs), mix of domestic and imported materials, some long-term contracts but spot exposure remains 30–50%, manageable disruption risk

Fully integrated or multi-source supply chain, critical inputs domestically secured, long-term linkage contracts (coal, gas, key chemicals), buffer inventory policy, no single supplier >15% of input cost

Metals & Mining (coking coal import dependency), Chemicals (crude-linked feedstocks), Automobile (semiconductor supply chain), Pharma (API import from China — 70% of KSMs), Power (coal linkage), Fertilizers (gas, phosphoric acid import), Electronics (component imports). Post-COVID supply chain resilience is a board-level agenda item.
Revenue directly controlled by government (subsidies, tariff orders, procurement), policy change can eliminate >30% of revenue, government is the primary customer, payment delays are structural
Government influence is material but manageable (<20% revenue from government/subsidized channels), some policy sensitivity but business can adapt, mixed private-government customer base
Minimal government dependency, fully market-driven pricing, private sector customer base, policy changes are tailwinds not threats (PLI scheme benefits, tax incentives, deregulation)

Fertilizers (100% subsidy-dependent), Power Distribution (tariff-regulated), Defence (government is sole customer), Construction/EPC (60%+ government orders), Agriculture (MSP, procurement), Railways (100% Indian Railways). Opposite end: IT Services, FMCG, Consumer Durables have near-zero government dependency. Score captures structural policy exposure, not current government friendliness.

High carbon emitter (>50 tCO2e/Cr revenue), water-intensive in water-stressed regions, hazardous waste generator, climate change directly threatens operations (agriculture, coastal infrastructure), CBAM exposure for exports
Moderate environmental footprint, manageable compliance costs (1–3% of revenue), transition pathway identified, renewable energy adoption >30%, some climate risk but not existential
Low environmental footprint (asset-light, digital), or actively part of the climate solution (renewable energy, EV, green hydrogen, waste management), ESG score attracts premium capital flows

Metals (steel = 7–9% of India's CO2), Cement (process emissions), Chemicals (hazardous waste), Power (coal plants), Oil & Gas (transition risk), Agriculture (monsoon/climate dependency). EU CBAM will directly impact Indian steel and aluminium exporters from 2026. Conversely, Renewable Energy, EV, Green Hydrogen, and Waste Management are structural beneficiaries of the climate transition.

Linear business model — revenue scales 1:1 with headcount/capex, no operating leverage, each incremental unit of revenue requires proportional cost, geographic expansion requires full capital replication
Moderate operating leverage — fixed cost base provides some scale benefits, 60–70% variable cost structure, expansion requires capital but at declining incremental cost

Highly scalable — platform/network effects, near-zero marginal cost of serving additional customers (SaaS, digital, exchanges), strong operating leverage (50%+ incremental margin), hub-and-spoke expansion model

Cloud & SaaS (near-zero marginal cost), Exchanges (BSE/NSE — infinite operating leverage), Digital Media & OTT, IT Services (partially scalable via offshore leverage), Insurance (once written, policies scale with minimal incremental cost). Opposite: BPO (headcount = revenue), Construction (each project is unique), Hospitals (bed capacity constrained), Agriculture (land-limited).
Market fully penetrated (>80% penetration), growth only from replacement/upgradation, TAM not expanding, market share gains require taking from entrenched competitors, GDP-linked growth at best
Moderate penetration (40–70%), growth from both new customers and existing customer upgrades, TAM expanding at 10–15% but competition for new customers is intensifying

Large under-penetrated market (<40% penetration vs. global benchmarks), India's per-capita gap creates 10–20 year structural runway, TAM expanding through formalization, digitization, urbanization, or rising incomes

Insurance (India 4% penetration vs. 7% global), Housing Finance (mortgage/GDP 12% vs. 50%+ developed), Healthcare (hospital beds/1000 at 0.5 vs. 3.0 global), Consumer Durables (AC penetration 8% vs. 40%+ China), Credit Cards (5% vs. 30%+ US). India's per-capita consumption gap across sectors is the single most powerful long-term investment thesis.
Severe talent shortage constraining growth, attrition >25%, specialized skills unavailable domestically, poaching war across industry, wage inflation >15% annually eroding margins
Adequate talent pool with competitive hiring, attrition 15–20%, specialized roles take 3–6 months to fill, wage growth in line with industry (8–12%), moderate training infrastructure

Deep talent pool (India produces relevant graduates at scale), strong employer brand attracting top talent, attrition <15%, robust training academies (TCS trains 40K freshers/year), talent is a competitive advantage not a constraint

IT Services (600K+ hires/year from India's engineering ecosystem), Pharmaceuticals (India's pharma R&D talent pool), Financial Services (chartered accountants, MBAs), Healthcare (doctor/nurse shortage is structural constraint), AI & Data Analytics (global competition for ML talent), Cybersecurity (talent deficit globally). Sectors where talent IS the product (IT, consulting, healthcare) must score this heavily.
SCORING INTEGRATION: HOW TO COMBINE LAYER 1 + LAYER 2
Score each of the 7 sector factors (1–10). Multiply each score by its weight. Sum to get Sector Qualitative Score (1–10 scale).
IT Sector: Regulatory 8 × 0.20 + Structural Demand 9 × 0.25 + Competitive 8 × 0.15 + Cyclicality 8 × 0.15 + Tech 9 × 0.10 + ESG 7 × 0.10 + Capital Access 9 × 0.05 = 8.30

Layer 1 captures the macro-environment that ALL industries within the sector operate in. A rising tide lifts all boats — a sector with strong structural demand, favorable regulation, and deep capital access provides a tailwind to every industry within it. 40% weight ensures the sector context sets the baseline, but doesn't dominate.
Select 5–7 factors from the Layer 2 menu most relevant to the industry. Assign weights (must total 1.00). Score each 1–10. Multiply and sum for Industry Qualitative Score.
IT Services: Mgmt Quality 8×0.20 + Switching Cost 7×0.20 + Talent 8×0.15 + Scalability 7×0.15 + Export 9×0.15 + Brand 6×0.15 = 7.55

Layer 2 is where differentiation happens. Two industries within the same sector (e.g., IT Services vs. BPO within IT) have very different qualitative profiles. Layer 2 captures these micro-level differences — talent dependency, IP moats, scalability, customer lock-in. 60% weight because industry-specific factors are ultimately more decisive for stock selection than sector-level macro.
Composite = (Layer 1 Score × 0.40) + (Layer 2 Score × 0.60). Result is the final Qualitative Score on 1–10 scale.

The composite balances sector context (40%) with industry specifics (60%). A strong sector with a weak industry (Power sector + stranded gas plants) gets pulled down. A weak sector with a strong industry (Textiles sector + technical textiles niche) gets lifted. The 40:60 split was chosen because industry selection within a sector typically explains more return variance than sector selection alone.
Deserves premium valuation (top-quartile P/E within sector). Qualitative moat justifies 20–40% premium over sector median multiple.
Quantitative score must confirm (ROCE > WACC, consistent growth). If quant is average but qual is exceptional — investigate if the moat hasn't yet reflected in financials (early-stage compounder).
Core portfolio candidate. Accumulate on dips. Hold through cycles. These are India's structural compounders.
HDFC Bank, TCS, Asian Paints, Bajaj Finance, Pidilite, Info Edge (Naukri), Avenue Supermarts (DMart), Titan
Fair-to-slight premium valuation. Qualitative supports paying 10–20% above sector median. Comfortable for institutional portfolios.
Quant must be at least sector-average. Strong qual + below-average quant = potential turnaround or overrated brand. Dig deeper into what's holding financials back.
Strong candidate. Build position if valuation is reasonable. Monitor for catalysts that could push to Band A (management upgrade, regulatory tailwind, TAM expansion).
Infosys, Kotak Bank, Sun Pharma, SRF, Divi's Labs, Crompton Greaves Consumer, ABB India, Tube Investments
Sector-median valuation appropriate. No qualitative premium or discount. Stock selection must rely primarily on quantitative factors and valuation discipline.
Quantitative factors dominate stock selection in this band. Strong quant + average qual = value opportunity if priced at discount. Weak quant + average qual = avoid.
Selective approach. Only invest when quantitative score is in top quartile AND valuation is at/below sector median. No qualitative margin of safety.
Wipro, Canara Bank, Grasim, Cipla, Apollo Hospitals, Tata Motors, UltraTech Cement, Godrej Consumer
Qualitative discount warranted. Apply 15–30% discount to sector median multiple. Only invest at deep value with strong quantitative conviction.
Even exceptional quant can't fully offset weak qual. If ROCE is 25%+ but governance is poor — returns accrue to promoter, not minority shareholders. Size positions smaller.
Tactical only. Requires catalyst for re-rating (management change, demerger, regulatory reform). Position sizing: max 2–3% of portfolio. Tight stop-loss discipline.
Vodafone Idea, SAIL, NHPC, IDBI Bank (improving), PNB, Jet Airways (post-restructuring), some PSU banks/companies with legacy governance issues
No valuation is cheap enough. Structural qualitative risks (sunset industry, governance fraud, regulatory destruction) mean intrinsic value is uncertain or declining. Value trap territory.
Quantitative irrelevant — financials may not be reliable (governance concerns), or business model is structurally broken regardless of current profitability. Cash flows may not reach minorities.
DO NOT INVEST. No position size is safe. These are permanent capital loss candidates. Avoid regardless of price, valuation, or short-term momentum.

Companies under SEBI investigation for fraud, coal mining stocks with stranded asset risk, print media with structural decline, companies with >80% promoter pledge and deteriorating business, shell companies
LAYER 2 FACTOR ASSIGNMENT GUIDE BY SECTOR

For each sector, the recommended Layer 2 qualitative factors and suggested weights are listed below. Industries within a sector may adjust weights ±5% based on their specific context, but the factor selection should remain consistent within a sector for comparability.

Re-score qualitative factors QUARTERLY (with results season). Layer 1 changes slowly (annual review usually sufficient). Layer 2 can shift faster (management change, regulatory event, competitive entry). Major events (RBI policy, budget, SEBI order) warrant immediate re-scoring of affected factors.

Score based on STRUCTURAL position, not last quarter's news. A single bad quarter doesn't change a company's moat. A single regulatory order doesn't change the sector's structural demand. Ask: 'Will this factor still matter in 3 years?' If yes, score it. If it's a one-off event, don't let it swing the score by more than 1 point.

A score of 7 in IT Services should reflect the SAME qualitative strength as a 7 in Banking. Calibrate across sectors by anchoring to the examples in the Score Interpretation bands above. If you score HDFC Bank's Management Quality as 9, and a mid-tier NBFC also at 9 — you've miscalibrated. Re-anchor.

The Qualitative Composite Score (1–10) feeds into the final stock scoring model alongside the Quantitative Score. Recommended integration: Final Score = (Quantitative Score × 0.60) + (Qualitative Composite × 0.40). This gives 60% weight to hard numbers and 40% to qualitative judgment — appropriate for Indian markets where governance, regulatory, and structural factors often override financial metrics.

For EVERY score you assign, write a 1–2 sentence justification. 'Regulatory Environment: 8 — SEBI's transparent framework, no adverse regulation in 3 years, MF SIP growth enabled by regulatory support.' This prevents score drift and enables audit trail when revisiting scores. Unjustified scores are worthless.
India's specialty chemicals market growing 12-15% (China+1 tailwind). Companies with 12-18% growth are gaining export share. SRF, PI Industries, Navin Fluorine lead.
Specialty chemicals earn 20-30% EBITDA (proprietary processes, custom synthesis). Commodity chemicals earn 10-14%. Product complexity determines margin sustainability.
Process chemistry and proprietary manufacturing routes are the moat. R&D >3% sustains innovation. PI Industries, SRF have strong proprietary chemistry capabilities.
Indian specialty chemicals benefit from China+1 sourcing. >40% exports = global competitiveness validation. PI Industries >70% export, Navin Fluorine ~55%.
Chemical manufacturing requires significant capex (reactors, utilities, effluent treatment). ROCE >18% is strong. SRF ~20%, PI ~25%. <12% = over-expanded or commodity-trapped.
Zero Discharge (ZLD); all CPCB norms met
Chemical plants face regulatory shutdowns for pollution. ZLD systems and clean compliance records are essential for operational continuity. CPCB/SPCB orders can halt production.

Specialty/commodity chemicals use crude-derived feedstocks (BTX, naphtha, ethylene). Crude oil price swings directly impact RM cost. Companies with backward integration or pass-through contracts are better insulated.
Export demand is growing faster than India's capacity. Companies with 30%+ expansion plans can capture share. Brownfield expansion (lower cost) preferred over greenfield.
India's per-capita chemical consumption is 1/10th of developed markets. Large distribution reach and rising domestic consumption are structural tailwinds for volume growth.

Commodity chemicals (soda ash, caustic soda, chlor-alkali, industrial gases) grow with GDP and industrial activity. India capacity expanding in soda ash (Tata Chemicals), chlor-alkali (Grasim). 10-15% growth captures both volume and price improvement.

Commodity chemicals are cyclical with margins linked to demand-supply balance. Trough margins ~10-12%, peak 18-22%. Through-cycle average >14% indicates cost leadership or integrated operations. Grasim (chlor-alkali) ~18-20%.

Commodity chemical plants have high fixed costs. Utilization >80% ensures cost absorption and profitability. Industry-wide utilization below 75% triggers price wars. Companies with captive demand (downstream integration) maintain higher utilization.

Energy is 30-40% of production cost for chlor-alkali, soda ash. Companies with captive power (coal/solar), gas access, and proximity to raw materials have structural cost advantages. Deepak Nitrite, NOCIL face crude-linked RM exposure.
Commodity chemicals are capital-intensive. ROCE >15% across the cycle indicates cost leadership. Grasim chlor-alkali ~18%. Below 12% at cycle peak means the asset is structurally uncompetitive.
Key RM captive or long-term contracted

Integrated producers (raw material → intermediate → finished chemical) capture more margin and are insulated from RM price volatility. Tata Chemicals' trona-based soda ash has natural cost advantage over synthetic soda ash producers.

India's specialty chemicals market is $35B+, growing 15-18% on China+1 tailwinds. PI Industries, SRF, Navin Fluorine, Aarti Industries lead. Growth above 18% signals market share gains in global CRAMS/CSM contracts.

Specialty chemicals earn 20-30% EBITDA due to proprietary processes and customer stickiness. PI Industries ~28%, SRF ~22%. Below 18% suggests the product portfolio is commoditizing or competition is intensifying.
R&D >4% of revenue; ≥5 new molecules in pipeline

Process chemistry innovation is the moat. R&D creates proprietary synthesis routes that competitors cannot easily replicate. PI Industries' CRAMS pipeline has 40+ molecules under development. Companies with <2% R&D spend risk commoditization within 5 years.

CRAMS (Custom Research and Manufacturing Services) for global innovators is the highest-quality revenue — long-term contracts, IP protection, sticky relationships. PI Industries derives >70% from CRAMS exports. Higher CRAMS share = defensible moat.

Specialty chemical capex is significant (multi-purpose reactors, clean rooms, effluent treatment). ROCE >20% validates that the IP-driven model generates adequate returns. SRF ~22%, PI ~25%. <15% means capex cycles are diluting returns.
CRAMS businesses often start with concentrated customer bases. Diversification across 10+ global innovators reduces single-client dependency. Loss of a top 3 client can create 10-15% revenue cliff.

Global innovators conduct rigorous ESG audits of Indian contract manufacturers. A single environmental or safety incident can lead to contract termination. Companies with ZLD, ISO 14001, and Responsible Care certification access premium contracts.

Petrochemicals profitability is driven by the spread between product price and feedstock cost. PE/PP spreads of $300-500/ton are healthy. RIL's O2C (oil-to-chemicals) segment tracks these spreads. Spread compression below $200 signals overcapacity cycles.

Petrochemical crackers are capital-intensive ($3-5B for a new ethylene cracker). High utilization (>85%) is essential for cost absorption. Indian utilization averages 85-90%. Shutdowns for maintenance (every 3-5 years) temporarily depress utilization.

Integrated complexes (refinery → cracker → polymer → downstream) capture more margin at each stage. RIL Jamnagar is the world's largest integrated refinery-petrochemical complex. Non-integrated players buying naphtha/ethylene face feedstock price volatility.

India imports 30-40% of its polymer demand. Domestic capacity additions (RIL, IOCL Paradip, HPCL Rajasthan) are import substitution opportunities. Revenue growth tracks volume expansion more than pricing in this cyclical sector.

Petrochemical expansion capex is massive. Debt/EBITDA <2.0x ensures the company can service debt through cyclical troughs. RIL has deleveraged significantly post-Jio/retail monetization. GAIL's petrochemical segment operates with minimal standalone debt.

Given massive capital employed, ROCE >12% across the cycle is adequate for petrochemicals. Cyclical peak ROCE can reach 20%+. Companies consistently below 10% are destroying value — the capital would earn more invested elsewhere.

Branded agrochemicals command 20-30% premium over generics. Companies with strong branded portfolios and export CRAMS pipelines earn higher and more stable margins. Domestic-vs-export split determines monsoon dependency.
Specialty agrochemicals and CRAMS earn 20-25% EBITDA margins. Fertilizers operate on thin, government-regulated margins of 8-14% (subsidy-dependent). Crude-linked intermediates create RM volatility.

Agrochemical product lifecycles are 10-15 years. Continuous new molecule development is essential. PI Industries' CRAMS pipeline is its primary growth driver. Companies with strong R&D have multi-year growth visibility.

Fertilizer companies carry massive government subsidy receivables (₹3,000-8,000 Cr). Delays create working capital strain and interest costs. DBT adoption is gradually improving cycles but unevenly implemented.

India has 120,000+ agrochemical dealers. Distribution depth determines market access. Companies with 50,000+ touchpoints have structural advantages. Strong last-mile reach in rural markets is a moat built over decades.

Capital efficiency varies widely. PI Industries ROCE ~22-25% (CRAMS + branded). Fertilizer companies struggle to earn above 10-12% due to regulated pricing and capital-intensive plants. Subsidy receivables inflate capital employed.

CIB registration is mandatory in India. Global registrations (US EPA, EU) take 3-5 years. Product bans can eliminate revenue overnight — India banned 27 pesticides in 2020. Companies with diversified portfolios are better insulated.

Carbon black demand tracks tire and rubber production (65% of end use). India's auto production growth + replacement tire demand drive 8-12% volume growth. Specialty carbon black (inks, plastics) grows faster at 12-15%.
CBFS cost pass-through with <30 day lag

Carbon black feedstock (CBFS) is a crude-oil derivative comprising 50-60% of production cost. Price pass-through mechanisms with tire OEMs (quarterly contracts) are critical. Companies like Phillips Carbon Black Ltd with rapid pass-through protect margins during crude volatility.

Carbon black earns 10-15% EBITDA margins at steady state. Specialty grades (conductive, high-structure) earn 15-20%. Dyes & pigments earn 14-22% depending on product complexity. Through-cycle margins matter more than peak.

Carbon black plants have high fixed costs and continuous process operations. >80% utilization is essential for cost absorption. India's installed capacity is ~2.2 MTPA with utilization at 75-85%. Capacity additions by PCBL are expanding Indian market.

Indian carbon black and dye producers benefit from cost arbitrage vs. China (environmental compliance costs). PCBL exports ~25-30%. Dye exporters (Atul, Bodal) supply 15-20% of global reactive/vat dye demand.

Carbon black and dyes are moderately capital-intensive. ROCE >15% indicates pricing discipline and cost efficiency. PCBL ~15-18%. Dye companies like Atul achieve higher ROCE (~20%) due to diversified specialty portfolio.

India produces ~400 MTPA cement (world’s 2nd largest). Construction materials demand is directly linked to housing, infrastructure, and urbanization. Companies growing volumes 8-12%+ are gaining market share.

Unit profitability metric normalizing for scale. Cement leaders earn ₹1,200-1,500/ton. Other construction materials (tiles, pipes, sanitaryware) target 15-20% EBITDA margins. Below thresholds signals cost inefficiency or pricing pressure.

Energy costs (35-40% for cement), logistics (25-30%), and raw materials drive construction material costs. Efficient producers minimize pet coke/coal dependency through WHR and proximity to raw material sources.
Construction materials have high operating leverage. 75-85% utilization is the sweet spot for cost absorption without supply glut. >85% gives pricing power. Industry averages ~70% for cement.
Construction materials are regional businesses due to high freight costs. Regional market share and dealer/distribution network depth determine pricing power and volume consistency.

Cyclical sector needs balance sheet strength. Debt/EBITDA <1.5x ensures companies can invest through downturns. Cement consolidation wave (Adani/Ambuja acquisition) shows deleveraging is key post-M&A.

Construction materials are capital-intensive (₹800-1,000 Cr per MTPA for cement). ROCE >15% justifies ongoing capex. Shree Cement ~20%, UltraTech ~14%. <12% means capacity isn’t earning its cost of capital.
India produces ~400 MTPA cement (world’s 2nd largest). Industry grows 8-10%. Companies at 12%+ are gaining market share via capacity addition or geographic expansion.
Unit profitability metric normalizing for scale. UltraTech ~₹1,200-1,400, Shree Cement ~₹1,500. <₹900/ton signals cost inefficiency or poor pricing in the region.

Energy (35-40% of cost), logistics (25-30%), and raw materials drive costs. Pet coke (crude-linked) is primary fuel — crude price swings directly impact costs. Companies with high WHR share (Shree Cement ~50%) and proximity to mines are better insulated.
Cement has high operating leverage. 75-85% utilization is the sweet spot (cost absorption without supply glut). Industry at ~70%. >85% = pricing power.
Blended cement ≥70%; avg lead distance <300 km
Blended cement (PPC, PSC) uses less clinker = lower energy cost. Shorter lead distance = lower freight cost. Both directly impact per-ton profitability.
Cement is cyclical. Low leverage (<1.5x) ensures survival during downcycles. UltraTech ~1x. >2.5x strains during price wars. Adani/Ambuja deleveraging after acquisition.
Cement is capital-intensive (₹800-1,000 Cr per MTPA). ROCE >15% is strong. Shree Cement ~20%, UltraTech ~14%. <12% means capacity isn’t earning its keep.

Other construction materials include ready-mix concrete, aggregates, AAC blocks, fly ash bricks, waterproofing products, and construction chemicals. India’s infrastructure push (smart cities, affordable housing, highways) drives 15%+ growth for organized players displacing unorganized market.

Construction chemicals (Pidilite, STP) earn 18-25% margins due to brand and formulation IP. Commodity materials (aggregates, sand, bricks) earn 8-12%. Product differentiation through brand, quality certification, and technical specifications drives margin premium.

Construction materials are distributed through dealer networks (retail) and direct institutional sales (builders, contractors, government projects). Wide distribution reach ensures consistent demand. Institutional sales are lumpy but higher volume.

Commodity construction materials face price competition. Companies with proprietary formulations, green products (low-carbon cement substitutes, recycled aggregates), and specialized solutions command premium pricing and higher margins. Value-added mix expansion is the margin improvement lever.

Construction materials involve dealer credit, inventory cycles, and project-linked receivables. NWC <60 days is efficient. Government project receivables can stretch to 90-120 days, straining cash flow for companies with high institutional sales.

Construction materials manufacturing is moderately capital-intensive. Companies with brand advantage (Pidilite, Astral) achieve 20-30% ROCE. Commodity producers earn 12-15%. ROCE below 12% signals undifferentiated product positioning.
EBITDA per ton strips out volume noise and shows true per-unit profitability. Indian steel cos. benchmark ₹8,000-12,000/ton; top quartile >12,000. Low EBITDA/ton = cost-curve vulnerability.
Cost position on the global curve determines survival in downturns. 1st/2nd quartile producers (Tata Steel, JSPL at lower end) survive even at cycle troughs.
Metals are price-takers — LME/CRU prices determine revenue ceiling. When commodity prices are above long-term average, margins expand; below average, only low-cost producers survive.
Highly cyclical sector — leverage kills in downturns. Debt/EBITDA <2.0x ensures solvency through commodity troughs. Indian steel deleveraged from 5x+ to <2x post-2020.
Cash flow confirms EBITDA is real and not stuck in inventory/receivables. CFO/EBITDA >70% is healthy; <50% signals working capital bloat from stockpiling.
Volume growth drives value in a commodity business. India's steel capacity target is 300 MTPA by 2030 (from ~165 MTPA). Companies expanding 10-20% over 3 years participate in this growth.
ROCE in metals is volatile (5-25% across cycles). Through-cycle ROCE >12% means the company creates value above its cost of capital even in downturns.

Energy (power, coking coal, fuel oil, natural gas) is 25-40% of metals production cost. Aluminium smelting alone uses 14,000-16,000 kWh per ton — power cost dominates. Steel needs coking coal ($200-400/ton, linked to crude/energy complex). Rising crude/gas prices flow through to higher power tariffs, fuel oil for kilns, and transport costs. This metric captures the true crude/gas exposure of metals companies — unlike GRM which is refining-specific. Companies with captive power (Hindalco, Vedanta) or long-term fuel linkages are better insulated.
Unit profitability metric. Tata Steel ₹12-15K/ton, JSW ~₹10-13K. <₹8,000/ton signals cost disadvantage. Integrated producers (own iron ore) have ₹3-5K/ton cost edge.
Cost = iron ore + coking coal + conversion. Integrated producers (Tata, JSPL with captive mines) at ₹30-35K/ton. Non-integrated at ₹40-45K/ton. Cost position = survival metric.
Steel has massive fixed costs. >85% utilization = healthy cost absorption. India industry at ~80%. >90% triggers expansion cycle and pricing power.
Steel is cyclical. Debt/EBITDA <2.5x ensures survival through downturns. Tata Steel ~1.5x (post-Europe), JSW ~1.8x. >3.5x = distress risk in downcycles.
Value-added steel (auto-grade, galvanized, electrical, coated) earns ₹5-15K/ton premium over commodity HR coils. Higher mix = margin stability through cycles.
Steel is among the most capital-intensive industries (₹5-7 Cr per ton of capacity). ROCE >12% through-cycle is strong. JSW ~14%, Tata ~12%.
Aluminium is an energy-intensive metal (35-40% cost is power). India’s Hindalco/Vedanta at $1,600-1,900/ton. Position on global cost curve determines profitability.
Revenue = LME price + regional premium. LME $2,200-2,600 range (2024). Companies selling at LME + $100-200 premium through quality and product mix.
Captive coal power at ₹3-4/unit vs. grid at ₹6-8/unit. >80% captive = massive cost advantage. Hindalco >90% captive. Renewable shift reduces ESG risk.
Flat-rolled products (auto, packaging, building) earn $300-500/ton premium over ingots. Hindalco’s Novelis (~60% of revenue) is the world’s largest aluminium rolling company.
Unit EBITDA at LME $2,300 = $400-600/ton for Indian smelters (low-cost power advantage). <$300/ton signals cost issues or LME downturn.
Aluminium smelting is extremely capital-intensive (₹20-25 Cr per ton of capacity). ROCE >10% through-cycle is acceptable. Hindalco ~12% including Novelis.
Custom smelters (Hindalco’s Birla Copper) earn TC/RC from miners for processing concentrate. Higher TC/RC = better smelter profitability. Market rates fluctuate with concentrate supply.
Copper smelting is continuous-process. >90% utilization = efficient operations. Hindalco Birla Copper at ~95%. <80% means unplanned shutdowns or raw material sourcing issues.
Copper cathode is commodity; value-added products (rods for cables, tubes for AC, strips for electronics) earn $200-400/ton premium. Higher VA mix = better margins.
Copper smelting produces sulphuric acid as byproduct (1.5 tons per ton of copper). At $80-120/ton, acid contributes 15-20% of smelter revenue. Key profitability swing factor.
Copper smelting is capital-intensive but less so than aluminium. ROCE >12% signals efficient operations. India’s copper capacity constrained post-Sterlite Tuticorin closure.
Hindustan Zinc is among the world’s lowest-cost zinc producers (~$1,000-1,100/ton). 1st quartile cost position ensures profitability across LME cycles.
Zinc LME price drives revenue directly. At $2,500/ton, Hindustan Zinc’s EBITDA margin exceeds 50%. Below $2,000 = margin compression but still profitable at 1st quartile cost.
Hindustan Zinc produces ~700 tons silver/yr as byproduct. Silver revenue provides natural hedge against zinc price volatility. Higher silver prices = bonus margin.
Mined ore reserves determine mine life. >15 years ensures long-term production visibility. Hindustan Zinc has ~25+ years reserve life in Rajasthan operations.
Integrated zinc miners (mine-to-metal) enjoy 45-55% EBITDA margins due to low cost position. Hindustan Zinc ~50%. <40% signals cost escalation or LME downturn.
Zinc mining is capital-moderate (underground mines). Hindustan Zinc ROCE >30% due to integrated operations and low cost. Among the highest ROCE in Indian metals.
AISC includes mining, processing, overhead, and sustaining capex. <$1,200/oz is globally competitive. India has limited gold mining (MOIL, Deccan Gold); mainly jewellery.
High positive correlation to gold prices
Gold miners’ profits leverage gold prices. At $2,000/oz with $1,200 AISC = $800/oz margin. For jewellery companies, gold price drives inventory gains/losses and consumer sentiment.
For jewellery cos (Titan, Kalyan), revenue growth reflects store expansion + same-store growth. India’s jewellery market ~$75B shifting from unorganized to organized retail.
Organized jewellery retail is under-penetrated (~35% market share). Titan (Tanishq) adding 40-50 stores/yr. Network expansion captures shift from local jewellers.
Jewellery retail runs on thin margins (7-10%) but high asset turns. Mining margins are high (40-55%) but India has minimal gold mining. Business model determines margin profile.
Titan’s ROCE ~25% despite thin margins (high asset turnover). Mining ROCE depends on grade and cost position. Efficient operators make it work at both ends.
Indian iron ore mining costs $15-30/ton ex-mine. NMDC ~$20/ton. Cost position determines profitability across commodity cycles. <$30 = profitable even at $80/ton iron ore.
Higher Fe content = premium pricing and preferred by steelmakers. 62% Fe is the benchmark grade. <58% Fe gets discount of $10-20/ton and faces demand issues.
India produces ~260 MT iron ore/yr (NMDC ~35 MT). Domestic steel expansion needs more ore. Companies growing production >8% capture this structural demand.
Mining leases in India are 50-year concessions (post-2015 auction). >15 years remaining ensures long-term production visibility. Lease expiry = production cliff risk.
Ore evacuation from mine to port/steelworks determines delivered cost. Rail-linked mines save ₹500-1000/ton vs. road. Slurry pipelines (NMDC) are even cheaper.
Mining is capital-intensive (equipment, infrastructure) but high-margin. NMDC ROCE ~18-22%. Captive mines within steel companies contribute to integrated ROCE.
Realization within 5% of LME benchmark; hedging on 20-40% of production

Zinc prices are LME-determined and highly cyclical ($2,000-4,000/ton range). Hindustan Zinc (India's monopoly zinc producer, Vedanta subsidiary) realizes LME price minus treatment charges. Price realization as % of LME benchmark indicates commercial efficiency. Hedging 20-40% of forward production provides downside protection without sacrificing upside. Zinc demand is driven by galvanizing (60% of use — corrosion protection for steel), die-casting, and batteries.
CoP in global bottom quartile (<$1,000/ton); declining trend

Hindustan Zinc is among the world's lowest-cost zinc producers at ~$1,000-1,100/ton (all-in sustaining cost). Low CoP position means the company remains profitable even at cycle troughs. CoP includes mining cost (underground vs open-pit), smelting (energy-intensive), and overheads. Companies in the bottom quartile globally survive full commodity cycles; top-quartile producers shut down during downturns. Mine grade (zinc content in ore) directly impacts CoP — higher grade = lower cost.
Proven + probable reserves ≥15 years of production; reserve replacement active

Mine life determines the long-term viability of the business. Hindustan Zinc's Rampura Agucha and Sindesar Khurd mines have 25+ year reserve lives. Companies actively exploring and replacing reserves (reserves replacement ratio >100%) ensure perpetuity. Declining ore grades over time increase CoP — companies investing in deeper mining technology and new deposit exploration offset this natural depletion.
Silver revenue ≥15% of total metal revenue; silver recovery improving

Zinc-lead ore typically contains 50-150 grams of silver per ton of ore. Hindustan Zinc is India's largest silver producer — silver revenue is a significant margin enhancer (15-20% of total revenue). At silver prices of $25-30/oz, by-product credits reduce effective zinc CoP by $150-250/ton. Companies with higher silver content in ore and better metallurgical recovery rates earn structurally higher margins than pure-play zinc miners without silver co-products.
EBITDA margin ≥40% at mid-cycle prices; ROCE ≥25%

Hindustan Zinc achieves EBITDA margins of 45-55% — among the highest in global metals due to low CoP and silver by-product credits. ROCE of 30-40% reflects the asset-light nature of mature mines (fully depreciated). Margin compression signals either falling zinc/silver prices or rising CoP. Compare margins at normalized mid-cycle zinc prices ($2,500-3,000/ton) for apples-to-apples assessment across cycles.
Zero fatality target; water recycling ≥80%; tailings management compliant

Underground zinc mining carries safety risks — fatality rates and lost-time injury frequency rates (LTIFR) are closely tracked. Tailings dam management is a critical environmental risk globally (Brumadinho disaster in Brazil). Companies with strong ESG metrics (water recycling, renewable energy in mining operations, progressive mine closure planning) face lower regulatory risk and access ESG-linked capital at preferential rates.
AISC <$1,200/oz for gold; positioned in global bottom half

AISC is the comprehensive cost metric including mining, processing, sustaining capex, G&A, and reclamation costs. At gold prices of $2,000-2,500/oz, companies with AISC <$1,200/oz earn $800+ margin per oz. India has limited listed gold miners (NMDC has minor gold operations). Globally, Barrick and Newmont benchmark at $1,000-1,300/oz AISC. Indian gold-related companies are primarily in refining (MMTC-PAMP), recycling, and gold-backed finance (Muthoot, Manappuram).
Production growth ≥5% YoY; reserves replacement ratio >100%

Gold production is measured in ounces (or kg for Indian companies). Reserve depletion is the existential risk for miners — companies must continuously explore and prove new reserves. India produces only ~1.5 tons of gold annually (negligible vs global 3,600 tons). Hutti Gold Mines (Karnataka, government-owned) is the only significant Indian gold mine. For gold-focused companies, production growth signals successful exploration and development of new deposits.
Revenue sensitivity modeled for ±15% gold price; hedge ratio 0-20%

Gold miners' earnings are highly leveraged to gold prices — a 10% move in gold price typically causes 20-30% move in EBITDA. Gold companies historically under-hedge (0-15%) because shareholders invest for gold price exposure. Companies with zero hedging offer maximum gold price leverage (Barrick unhedged). Indian gold finance companies (Muthoot, Manappuram) have different gold price exposure — rising gold increases their LTV headroom and loan book growth potential.
Gold recovery ≥92%; improving through metallurgical innovation

Recovery rate = gold actually extracted / gold contained in ore. Modern processing achieves 90-95% recovery. Refractory ores (where gold is locked in sulfide minerals) require more complex processing (roasting, pressure oxidation) and achieve lower recovery. Companies investing in processing technology to improve recovery by even 1-2 percentage points generate significant incremental revenue at zero mining cost.

Gold mining EBITDA margins are highly cyclical — 50%+ at peak gold prices, 15-20% at troughs. The margin structure determines whether the company can sustain dividends, exploration spending, and development capex through the cycle. Companies that maintain positive FCF even at $1,500/oz gold demonstrate operational resilience and conservative cost management.
Conflict-free gold certification; mercury-free processing; community development spend ≥1% of revenue

Gold mining faces intense ESG scrutiny — mercury use in artisanal mining, cyanide in processing, community displacement, and conflict mineral sourcing. Responsible gold certifications (LBMA Good Delivery, Fairmined) command market premiums and access institutional investors. Companies with strong ESG practices face lower permitting risk, community opposition, and regulatory challenges. Indian refiners sourcing from responsible supply chains (MMTC-PAMP) differentiate in export markets.
Portfolio includes ≥3 critical minerals (as per India's Critical Mineral Strategy)

India identified 30 critical minerals in 2023 — lithium, cobalt, rare earths, graphite, titanium, tungsten, vanadium. Indian Rare Earths Ltd (IREL, government) is the primary producer. KABIL (Khanij Bidesh India Ltd) is acquiring overseas mining rights. Companies producing or processing minerals on India's critical list have strategic value beyond commercial metrics — national security and EV supply chain considerations drive government support through mining leases, PLI benefits, and import duty protection.
Downstream processing (refining/separation) capability; not just mining

Rare earth mining is relatively straightforward; separation and refining into individual elements (neodymium, dysprosium, lanthanum) is the high-value, high-barrier step. China controls 85%+ of global rare earth processing. Indian companies with separation and refining capabilities (IREL's rare earth separation plant at Aluva, Kerala) capture significantly more value than raw ore exporters. Processing capability is the true competitive moat — it takes 5-10 years and ₹1,000+ Cr to build a separation facility.
Revenue from EV/defense/electronics supply chain ≥40%; growing faster than traditional markets

Rare earths and specialty minerals are critical for EV motors (neodymium magnets), wind turbines, defense systems (guided missiles, fighter jets), and electronics (smartphones, semiconductors). Companies aligned with these high-growth end-use markets have superior demand visibility. The EV transition alone is expected to increase rare earth demand by 5-7x by 2035. Companies supplying into defense/space programs also benefit from government procurement guarantees.
Proven reserves ≥20 years; government mining lease secured

Specialty mineral deposits are geographically concentrated globally. India has significant deposits of rare earths (Kerala/Tamil Nadu beach sands — monazite, ilmenite), lithium (Jammu & Kashmir), graphite (Jharkhand, Odisha), and titanium (Kerala, Odisha). Companies with long-term mining leases (typically 30-50 years from government) and proven reserves have multi-decade resource security. The mine auction process in India creates high barriers to entry for new competitors.
Revenue CAGR ≥20%; benefiting from domestic sourcing mandates

India's critical mineral strategy includes: (a) 100% FDI in mining, (b) royalty concessions for critical minerals, (c) PLI for downstream processing, (d) KABIL overseas acquisitions. Companies positioned to benefit from these policies see accelerated growth. Revenue growth should be measured against policy milestones — domestic sourcing mandates for EVs (localization requirements under PLI for Advanced Chemistry Cell batteries) create guaranteed demand for Indian-sourced critical minerals.
Radioactive material handling compliance (AERB); environmental clearances current

Rare earth mining (especially from monazite beach sands) involves radioactive thorium as a by-product. AERB (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board) compliance is mandatory. Environmental clearances for mining in ecologically sensitive coastal areas are stringent. Companies with proper radioactive waste management, environmental monitoring, and community engagement programs face lower regulatory risk. International customers increasingly require responsible sourcing certifications for critical minerals.
Pulp Price Sensitivity & Raw Material Mix
Captive plantation/waste paper ≥60% of fiber source

Paper manufacturing is pulp-intensive — fiber cost is 40-50% of production cost. Companies with captive plantations or high waste paper recycling have structural cost advantages over imported pulp buyers ($600-1,000/ton, volatile).

Commodity writing/printing paper is mature (2-4% growth). Growth segments: packaging board (8-12% growth driven by e-commerce), specialty papers (thermal, security, medical), and tissue paper (15%+ growth). Higher-value mix improves margins.
Integrated mills (pulp + paper at same site with captive plantations) earn 18-25% EBITDA. Standalone mills buying market pulp earn 10-16%. Margin cycles follow global pulp prices with 3-6 month lag.

Paper mills are capital-intensive (₹4,000-6,000 Cr for 200,000 TPA). High utilization (>85%) is essential for profitability. India’s installed capacity is ~25 million TPA. Brownfield expansion preferred for lower capex.

Paper faces environmental scrutiny — deforestation, water usage (100-150 m³/ton), effluent discharge. FSC certification is increasingly required by global FMCG clients. Companies with sustainable forestry and closed-loop water systems access premium customers.

Paper manufacturing is highly capital-intensive with long asset lives (30+ years). ROCE of 15-20% for well-run integrated mills is achievable. New capacity should generate returns above WACC (12-13%) within 3 years.
Captive plantation/waste paper ≥60% of fiber; imported pulp <30%

Fiber cost is 40-50% of production cost. ITC Paperboards has 250,000+ acres of social/farm forestry. JK Paper uses high waste paper recycling. Captive sources provide structural cost advantage vs. imported pulp ($600-1,000/ton, volatile).

Commodity writing/printing paper grows 2-4%. Packaging board (8-12% growth, e-commerce driven), specialty papers (thermal, security), and tissue (15%+ growth) are higher-margin. ITC’s paperboard business is a margin leader.

Integrated mills earn 18-25%. Standalone earn 10-16%. Energy cost (coal/biomass boilers) is 15-20% of production. Companies with WHR and biomass boilers have lower costs. Margins follow global pulp prices with 3-6 month lag.

Capital-intensive (₹4,000-6,000 Cr for 200,000 TPA new mill). JK Paper’s Sirpur acquisition and expansion to 1M TPA demonstrates brownfield strategy — lower capex and faster commissioning. Companies expanding in packaging board have clearer demand visibility.
FSC/PEFC certified; water recycling ≥80%; carbon neutral target

Paper faces scrutiny on deforestation, water usage (100-150 m³/ton), and effluent. FSC certification increasingly required by global FMCG clients for packaging. ITC’s ‘Wellbeing Out of Waste’ program is a sector benchmark.

Highly capital-intensive with long asset lives (30+ years). ROCE of 15-20% is achievable for well-run integrated mills. New capacity should earn above WACC within 3 years. JK Paper’s post-acquisition ROCE improvement demonstrates value-accretive M&A.

India is the world’s largest jute producer (~60% global output). Mandatory jute packaging for food grains (Jute Packaging Materials Act) provides baseline demand. Diversified jute products (geo-textiles, composites, lifestyle bags) drive incremental growth.
Raw jute cost <55% of revenue; multi-source procurement

Raw jute prices are volatile (monsoon-dependent, West Bengal concentrated). Companies with diversified procurement across Bangladesh/Bihar/Assam and inventory management strategies have more stable margins. MSP for raw jute provides some floor.

Commodity jute sacking earns 5-8% margins (government-controlled pricing). Diversified jute products (geo-textiles for road construction, decorative jute, composites) earn 12-20%. Product diversification is the path to margin improvement.

Government mandated sacking is low-margin but provides volume base. Companies pivoting to geo-textiles (road construction demand), jute-based composites (auto/construction), and lifestyle products earn higher margins and reduce policy dependency.

Jute Packaging Materials Act mandates jute bags for food grains and sugar. Policy changes (dilution of mandatory packaging norms) directly impact demand. Companies with lower government-mandated revenue are more resilient to policy risk.

Jute mills are aging assets (many 50+ years old). Modernized mills with updated machinery achieve better ROCE. Capital efficiency depends on product mix — value-added products generate higher returns on the same capital base.

Forest products include timber, plywood, MDF, particle board, and wood-based panels. India’s plywood market is ₹30,000+ Cr growing at 10-12%. Century Plyboards and Greenply are leaders. Real estate recovery and interior fitout demand drive growth.

Organized plywood (Century, Greenply) earns 13-16% EBITDA. Unorganized sector (60%+ market share) operates on thin margins. GST implementation has been a structural tailwind shifting share to organized players who can claim input tax credit.
Domestic + imported timber diversified; no single source >40%

Timber is the primary raw material. India imports significant timber (Myanmar, Gabon, New Zealand). Companies with diversified sourcing across domestic plantations and multiple import origins mitigate supply disruption risk and price volatility.

Plywood is increasingly a branded category. Century Ply and Greenply invest in brand building, driving specification demand from architects and contractors. Wider dealer networks ensure product availability across Tier 2/3 cities.
MDF + laminates + particle board >30% of revenue

Plywood is the core product but MDF (medium-density fiberboard) is growing faster (20%+ vs. 10% for plywood). Companies expanding into MDF, laminates, and particle board capture the shift toward engineered wood products in modern furniture.

Forest products manufacturing is moderately capital-intensive (MDF plants: ₹500-800 Cr). ROCE >15% signals efficient capital use. Century Ply ~18%, Greenply ~14%. Capacity expansion should be demand-led to avoid utilization drop.
Discretionary sector — volumes prove genuine demand vs. price hikes. Indian auto OEMs target 10-15% volume growth. Market share gains matter more than industry growth rate.
OEM margins vary by segment. Maruti ~12-14%, M&M ~15-17%, Bajaj Auto ~18-20%. Component margins depend on complexity: forgings/castings ~12-14%, electronics ~15-20%.
Top 3 in segment; top OEM client <40%
Auto is a scale business. Top 3 players enjoy distribution leverage, supplier bargaining power, and brand recognition. For components, diversification across OEMs reduces volume cliff risk.
Auto manufacturing is capital-intensive (plants, tooling, R&D). ROCE >18% signals efficient capital use. Maruti ~25%, Bajaj ~35%. <15% means sub-scale or poor model/product mix.
Export markets provide diversification and USD revenue. Bajaj exports ~45% of 2W production. India is a global auto component hub (~$21B/yr exports). >20% reduces India-cycle dependency.

EV transition creates both risk (engine components become obsolete) and opportunity (battery, power electronics, thermal management). Companies pivoting to EV content early gain first-mover advantage.
Cash locked in inventory and receivables is cash not earning returns. <60 days net WC cycle is efficient; >90 days signals channel stuffing or slow collections.
Volume growth proves real demand. Indian PV market ~4.3M units/yr growing 8-12%. Two-wheelers ~18M units. Market share gains matter more than industry growth rate.
OEM margins vary by segment. Maruti ~12-14%, M&M ~15-17%, Bajaj Auto ~18-20% (premium 2W). Below threshold signals commodity cost pressure or competitive discounting.
Auto is a scale business. Top 3 players in each segment (PV, 2W, CV) enjoy distribution leverage, supplier bargaining power, and brand recognition. Market share is the key moat.
High fixed costs in manufacturing. >80% utilization ensures cost absorption. At 90%+, OEMs need new capacity (capex cycle). <70% means demand weakness or overcapacity.
Auto manufacturing is capital-intensive (plants, tooling, R&D). ROCE >20% signals efficient capital use. Maruti ~25%, Bajaj ~35%. <15% means sub-scale or poor model mix.
Strong order backlog (>2 months) indicates demand exceeds supply — pricing power intact. <1 month backlog means demand softening or production overcapacity.
Export markets provide diversification and USD revenue. Bajaj exports ~45% of 2W production. >20% exports reduces India-cycle dependency.
Revenue growth should exceed OEM volume growth (indicates content-per-vehicle increase). India auto component industry ~$70B growing 12-15%. Export share adds growth vector.
Margins depend on product complexity. Forgings/castings ~12-14%, electronics ~15-20%. Commodity component makers face margin pressure from OEM cost negotiations.
OEM business has volume but squeezed margins (buyer power). Aftermarket carries 2-3x higher margins and recurring demand. >30% aftermarket = balanced, higher-margin mix.
India is a global auto component export hub (~$21B/yr). >25% exports = diversification + USD earnings. Companies supplying global OEMs (Motherson, Sundaram) have stickier revenue.
Auto components are moderately capital-intensive. ROCE >18% is healthy. Bosch ~20%, Motherson ~15%. <12% signals capacity underutilization or poor pricing power.
Over-dependence on one OEM creates volume cliff risk if that OEM loses market share. Diversified across ≥4 OEMs + aftermarket is safer.
EV transition is a risk (engine components become obsolete) and opportunity (battery cooling, power electronics). Companies pivoting to EV content early gain first-mover advantage.
Fixed-cost leverage — higher utilization spreads overheads and directly boosts margins. <65% means assets are underutilized and dragging RoCE.
Cash locked in inventory and receivables is cash not earning returns. <60 days net WC cycle is efficient; >90 days signals channel stuffing or slow collections.
Capital-intensive sector needs high ROCE to justify investment. Indian durables leaders average 18-25%; <15% means capital is not being deployed productively.
Moat metric — wide dealer/retail network creates switching costs and service accessibility. Direct-to-consumer (DTC) channel adds margin and data advantages.
Cyclical sector needs balance sheet strength to survive downturns. Debt/Equity <0.5x ensures companies can self-fund through slow demand periods.
India’s consumer electronics market ~$12B growing at 12-15%. Rising incomes + low penetration (AC ~10%, dishwasher <2%) = structural multi-year growth opportunity.
Electronics margins are thin due to global competition. EBITDA >10% is strong (Dixon ~7-8% but growing via PLI). Premium brands (Sony, Samsung) earn higher.
Category leadership drives retail shelf space and brand recall. Havells #1 in fans, Voltas #1 in room AC. Market leadership = pricing power in a commoditized market.
Govt PLI scheme (AC, LED, mobile) provides 4-6% incentive on incremental production. Companies enrolled and meeting targets get direct margin uplift.
Omnichannel presence is critical. Online captures 30-40% of electronics now. Companies without strong online (Amazon, Flipkart) presence lose volume to D2C brands.
Electronics is moderately capital-intensive (assembly lines, R&D). ROCE >15% is healthy. Dixon ~18%, Havells ~22%. <12% = overinvestment without corresponding revenue.
White goods penetration in India is extremely low (AC ~10%, washing machines ~15%, refrigerators ~35%). 15-20% volume growth is structural as urbanization and incomes rise.
White goods margins are under pressure from raw materials (copper, steel, compressors). EBITDA >12% indicates pricing power and component cost control. Voltas ~10-12%, Blue Star ~8-10%.
Premium (inverter AC, smart appliances) carries higher margins. >30% premium mix signals brand positioning strength and customer willingness to pay for technology.
After-sales service is critical for white goods (installation, AMC, repairs). Wide service network improves customer experience and creates recurring revenue from AMCs.
Manufacturing is seasonal (AC = summer peak). Annual utilization >75% across product lines ensures cost efficiency. <65% means overcapacity or wrong product mix.
White goods manufacturing is capital-intensive. ROCE >18% is strong. Whirlpool India ~20%, Voltas ~18%. <14% signals either capacity drag or margin compression.
Leisure/lifestyle is a premiumization play (sports equipment, musical instruments, hobby goods). Rising discretionary income in India supports 15-20% growth.
Leisure products carry brand premium. Gross margin >40% indicates product differentiation. <35% suggests commoditized products competing on price.
IP (brands, patents, exclusive distribution) is the moat. Companies with own brands or exclusive India rights for global brands have defensible market positions.
Leisure products are seasonal and inventory-heavy. Working capital <90 days is efficient; >120 days means slow-moving inventory or poor collection from distributors.
Niche market with moderate capex. ROCE >15% justifies capital deployed. <12% means the brand premium isn’t translating to returns.
Home furnishing in India is shifting from unorganized to organized sector. Real estate recovery and premiumization drive 12-18% growth for branded players.
Branded home furnishing (tiles, sanitaryware, paints-adjacent) earns 40-55% gross margins. Asian Paints (home decor) ~42%, Kajaria ~50%. <35% = commoditized.
Fragmented market where brand trust matters (high-value purchase, infrequent buying cycle). Wide dealer network + strong brand = sustainable market share.
Home furnishing involves inventory across SKUs and slow-moving designs. Working capital <75 days indicates efficient inventory management and dealer collections.
Moderately capital-intensive (manufacturing + showrooms). EBITDA >14% signals brand premium and efficient operations. Kajaria ~18%, Somany ~10%.
Capital deployed in manufacturing plants and brand building. ROCE >18% is healthy. <14% indicates overcapacity or margin compression from competition.
India’s EV market is at inflection. EV 2W growing 40%+, 4W <3% penetration. Companies growing >40% YoY are capturing the S-curve adoption phase.
Battery is 40% of EV cost. <$120/kWh enables price parity with ICE vehicles. India targets $100/kWh by 2027. Companies with cell manufacturing (Ola, Tata) have cost advantage.
Most Indian EV makers are pre-profit or thin margin. >15% gross margin signals viable unit economics. Path to 20%+ at scale is critical for long-term sustainability.
Range anxiety is the #1 adoption barrier. >300 km range and fast-charging capability addresses this. Companies with own charging networks (Tata, Ather) have ecosystem advantage.
Strong booking pipeline signals demand exceeds supply — pricing power and growth visibility. Tata EV bookings routinely exceed 3-month production capacity.
Most EV companies are pre-profit and burning cash for growth. Cash runway >3 years ensures they survive to profitability without distressed capital raises.
