SSSG isolates organic growth from new store expansion. Titan achieves 15-20% SSSG driven by brand trust and premiumization. Kalyan Jewellers targets 12-15%. SSSG decomposition into volume growth vs gold price appreciation is critical — pure price-led growth (from gold price rally) is less sustainable than volume/mix-led growth. Negative SSSG signals demand weakness or competitive market share loss.
Revenue CAGR ≥18%; net store additions ≥15% YoY

India's organized jewellery market is ₹5 lakh Cr+ and growing as unorganized-to-organized shift accelerates post-GST and hallmarking mandate. Titan (Tanishq) has ~2,000 stores; Kalyan expanding aggressively with franchise model. Revenue growth is driven by store count expansion × SSSG × gold price appreciation. Companies growing stores at 15-20% annually while maintaining positive SSSG demonstrate scalable execution.
Studded/diamond share ≥30% of sales; making charges ≥12% of gold jewellery

Gold jewellery earns 8-12% making charges (low margin on high gold value). Studded/diamond jewellery earns 30-50% gross margins — 4x the margin of plain gold. Companies with higher studded ratio (Titan at ~30%, increasing) have structurally superior margins. Making charge realization (%) indicates pricing power and brand premium. The industry trend is toward higher studded mix as aspirational consumers upgrade.

Jewellery companies hold ₹5,000-15,000 Cr of gold inventory. Unhedged gold positions create P&L volatility — a 5% gold price swing on ₹10,000 Cr inventory is ₹500 Cr impact. Titan's gold-on-lease model minimizes balance sheet gold exposure. Kalyan hedges through commodity exchanges. Companies with disciplined hedging policies and lean inventory days generate more predictable earnings and better capital efficiency.
Franchise vs Owned Mix & Unit Economics
Franchise stores achieving payback <3 years; owned store EBITDA ≥8%

The jewellery retail model is shifting to asset-light franchise expansion. Kalyan's FOCO (Franchise Owned, Company Operated) model targets ₹3.5 Cr investment per store with 2.5-year payback. Titan operates company-owned stores with higher control but more capital. Unit economics per store (revenue per sqft, EBITDA per store) determine scalability. Companies achieving healthy franchise unit economics can scale faster without proportional capital deployment.

Jewellery retail operates on thin EBITDA margins (6-10%) but high asset turnover, resulting in strong ROCE (Titan Jewellery at ~30% ROCE). Margin is sensitive to gold price hedging effectiveness, studded ratio, making charge realization, and operating leverage from store expansion. ROCE is the better profitability metric as it accounts for the capital-intensive gold inventory. Companies maintaining ROCE >20% while expanding store network are creating value.
Volume growth ≥10% YoY; outpacing industry average

India's paint industry is ₹70,000+ Cr growing at 10-12% by volume. Volume growth (litres sold) is the core metric — isolates real demand growth from price increases. Asian Paints leads with ~33% market share. New entrants (Grasim/Birla Opus, JSW Paints) are disrupting with aggressive pricing. Companies growing volume faster than the 10% industry average are gaining share. Volume growth decomposition: repainting cycle (4-5 years) × new construction × premiumization.

Paint margins are driven by crude-linked raw materials (TiO2, monomers, solvents account for 55-65% of cost), operating leverage, and pricing power. Asian Paints maintains 18-22% EBITDA margin through superior RM procurement, dealer management, and brand premium. Berger achieves 15-18%. New entrants (Grasim) are operating at negative/thin margins to gain share. Margin trends over 4-6 quarters show whether RM cost pass-through is effective.
Dealer network >25,000; tinting machine share >70% of points of sale

Distribution is the ultimate moat in paints. Asian Paints has ~1.5 lakh dealers (untouchable). Berger has ~40,000. Tinting machines at dealer shops create lock-in — once a dealer has a brand's machine, switching is costly. Companies with deeper tier-2/3/rural distribution access under-penetrated markets growing at 15-20%. Dealer loyalty programs, credit terms, and tinting machine technology (colour-matching accuracy) differentiate brands.
Decorative ≥70% of revenue for retail-focused companies

Decorative paints (home/office) account for 75% of India's paint market with 15-22% EBITDA margins. Industrial paints (automotive OEM, protective coatings) earn 10-15% margins but provide counter-cyclical stability. Companies with higher decorative mix have higher margins and consumer brand equity. Kansai Nerolac's automotive paint business provides industrial stability but lower blended margins. Premium decorative (waterproofing, wood finishes, textures) earns 25-30% margins.
New product revenue ≥15% of sales; R&D ≥1.5% of revenue

Paint innovation drives premiumization — waterproofing solutions (Asian Paints SmartCare), anti-bacterial paints (post-COVID demand), wood finishes, and texture/designer finishes command 30-50% price premiums over base emulsions. R&D also drives cost optimization (better formulations with lower TiO2 loading). Companies with strong innovation pipelines convert painters/contractors into brand advocates through training programs and app-based colour visualization tools.

Paints is a high-ROCE business — moderate fixed assets (manufacturing plants) + negative working capital (dealer advance payments) + strong pricing power = exceptional capital efficiency. Asian Paints achieves ROCE of 28-35%. Berger at 22-28%. New entrants (Grasim, JSW) will have depressed ROCE during investment phase but must demonstrate a path to 18%+ within 5 years. Declining ROCE for incumbents signals competitive margin pressure from new entrants.
India's textile market ~$150B. PLI scheme, China+1, and branded retail drive growth. Companies at 12-18% are capturing both domestic and export opportunities.
Textile manufacturing (yarn, fabric) earns 10-14% EBITDA. Branded apparel (Page Industries, Trent) earns 18-25%. Business model determines margin profile.
Export-heavy companies face order volatility and USD/INR risk. Domestic-heavy miss global opportunities. Balanced mix provides diversification and growth optionality.
Commodity textiles (yarn, gray fabric) face price volatility. Value-added products (branded apparel, technical textiles, home textiles) earn 2-3x margins.
Textiles are working capital intensive (raw cotton inventory, long production cycles). <90 days is efficient; >120 days means cash is locked in inventory or receivables.
Textiles are capital-intensive (spinning mills, weaving, processing). ROCE >15% is strong for manufacturers. Page Industries ~35% (branded). <10% = commodity trap.

India's textile & apparel market ~$150B. PLI scheme for MMF/technical textiles, China+1 sourcing shift, and domestic branded retail growth drive 12-18% revenue expansion. Trent, Page Industries lead domestic; Gokaldas, KPR Mill lead exports.
Commodity yarn/fabric earns 10-14% EBITDA. Branded apparel (Page ~20%, Trent ~10% scaling) earns premium. Companies moving up from manufacturing to branded retail capture structurally higher margins.

Export-heavy faces order volatility and USD/INR risk. Domestic-heavy misses global China+1 shift. Balanced mix provides counter-cyclical diversification. India's textile exports ~$35B targeting $100B by 2030.
Commodity textiles (yarn, gray fabric) face raw cotton price volatility. Branded apparel, home textiles (Welspun), and processed fabrics earn 2-3x margins and provide pricing power.

Raw cotton inventory (seasonal procurement), long production cycles, and export receivables (60-90 day LCs) lock capital. <90 days NWC is efficient; >120 signals inventory buildup or buyer payment delays.

Spinning mills and processing plants are capital-intensive. ROCE >15% is strong. Page Industries ~35% (branded asset-light). KPR Mill ~18% (vertically integrated). <10% = commodity trap with no pricing power.

Technical textiles (geo-textiles, agro-textiles, medical textiles, industrial fabrics) growing 18-20% driven by infrastructure spend (roads need geo-textiles), healthcare demand, and automotive applications. India's technical textile market ~$22B, low penetration vs. global.

Technical textiles earn premium margins (16-22%) vs. commodity textiles (10-14%) due to specialized manufacturing, product certification requirements, and lower competition. Companies with proprietary processes and certifications maintain margin stability.
R&D >3% of revenue; international certifications active

Technical textiles require product certification (BIS, ISO, customer-specific testing). Certified products create entry barriers. R&D investment in new applications (fire-retardant, anti-microbial, high-tenacity) drives product premiumization.

Technical textiles span 12 application areas (geo-tech, medi-tech, agro-tech, pack-tech, etc.). Diversification across applications reduces cyclical risk. SRF and Garware Technical Fibres are diversified leaders.
Global technical textile market ~$200B. India's share is growing with China+1 sourcing. Companies exporting >30% benefit from USD pricing, diverse demand sources, and global quality benchmarks.

Technical textile plants are moderately capital-intensive but earn higher margins than commodity textiles. ROCE >18% is achievable for specialized manufacturers. SRF ~20%. Companies with backward integration into polymers earn even higher returns.
≥15% YoY; subscription revenue growing faster than ad revenue

Media revenue comes from two streams: advertising (cyclical, linked to GDP/adex growth) and subscription (recurring, stickier). India's media industry is ~₹2.4L Cr and growing at 8-10% CAGR. Companies shifting mix toward subscription (OTT, paid digital) have more predictable revenue. Ad revenue is volatile — it surges during IPL/elections and crashes during economic slowdowns.

Content is the single largest cost for media companies — original programming, sports rights (IPL rights alone cost ₹48,000 Cr over 5 years), and movie acquisitions. Companies that own content libraries (Sun TV, Tips Industries) have lower marginal content cost vs those that must continually acquire/produce (Zee, OTT platforms). Amortization of content costs is a key accounting judgment — aggressive amortization depresses near-term profits but is conservative.

Operating leverage is strong in media — once content is created, distributing it to 10M vs 100M viewers costs marginally more. Sun TV operates at >50% EBITDA margin because it owns regional content with near-zero incremental cost. OTT platforms are typically loss-making initially but margins improve dramatically at scale (Netflix global ~25%, Indian OTT still mostly negative).
Viewership share growing; DAU/MAU >30% (digital); GRP leadership

Audience reach directly drives ad revenue — advertisers pay per eyeball. For TV, GRP (Gross Rating Points) and viewership share (BARC data) determine ad rates. For digital, DAU/MAU ratio measures engagement quality — a platform with 100M users but 20% daily engagement is worth less than one with 50M users and 50% DAU/MAU. Content virality and retention metrics are the digital equivalent of TV ratings.
Digital revenue >30% of total; growing >25% YoY

India's digital advertising market is growing at 25-30% CAGR while traditional (TV, print) grows at 5-8%. Companies successfully transitioning to digital (Zee5, JioCinema, Times Internet) command higher valuation multiples. Digital revenue is subscription + digital ads — both are higher-margin than traditional distribution. Print companies without digital pivot face structural decline.
Owned content library; evergreen IP generating recurring royalty

Content libraries are the ultimate moat in media — they generate revenue perpetually with zero incremental cost. Tips Industries and Saregama earn royalties from decades-old music catalogues. Sun TV's library of 30,000+ hours of Tamil/Telugu content is irreplaceable. Companies that own IP (vs license it) have structurally higher margins and more predictable cash flows. In the OTT era, library content drives 60-70% of viewing time even though new originals get the headlines.

Media is asset-light (no factories, minimal fixed assets beyond studios/equipment) so ROCE should be high. Sun TV achieves >30% ROCE, Zee historically ~15-18%. Low ROCE in an asset-light media business signals poor capital allocation — typically from overpaying for content rights, failed OTT investments, or acquisitions that don't generate returns.
Growing faster than industry adex; premium pricing

GRP (Gross Rating Points) is the currency of TV advertising. Revenue per GRP measures pricing power — channels with top-rated shows command premium CPM (cost per mille). Star Sports earns 3-5x the CPM of general entertainment due to IPL. Consistent GRP leadership in prime-time slots drives ad revenue without proportional cost increase.

NTO 2.0 regime (TRAI regulation) made a-la-carte channel selection mandatory, changing the economics from bouquet-based to per-channel pricing. Higher ARPU = stronger content = higher willingness to pay. Regional broadcasters (Sun TV) have stickier subscription revenue because of language/cultural barriers to entry.

The single largest expense. Original programming, sports rights, and movie acquisitions. Companies with owned content libraries (Sun TV's 30,000+ hours) have near-zero marginal content cost. Those bidding for sports rights (IPL, Olympics) face escalating costs every cycle. Content cost efficiency separates profitable broadcasters from cash-burning ones.
Top 3 in target genre/language; GRP share >15%

Viewership share directly correlates with ad revenue share — broadcasters command ad rates proportional to their audience share. BARC (Broadcast Audience Research Council) data is the industry standard. Consistent share gains signal content resonance; persistent losses signal audience migration to OTT.

India has ~200M TV households. Reach determines the addressable audience for advertisers. Companies with strong distribution across cable, DTH, and OTT have multi-platform monetization. DD Free Dish (45M homes) is a massive free-to-air platform that low-cost channels use for reach.

Linear TV viewership in urban India is declining as audiences shift to OTT. Broadcasters must transition their audience to digital platforms (Zee5, SonyLIV, Hotstar). Those lagging in digital will see accelerating revenue decline as advertisers shift digital budgets.
Broadcasting is asset-light (studios, transponder costs, content). ROCE should be >18% for well-run broadcasters. Low ROCE signals overspending on content rights or failed digital ventures.

OTT economics depend on paid subscriber scale. India has ~100M paid OTT subscriptions (many users subscribe to multiple platforms). Paid sub growth — not free/ad-supported — is the metric that drives ARPU and path to profitability. JioCinema's freemium-to-paid conversion post-IPL is the key test case.

Indian OTT ARPU is among the lowest globally (₹100-200/month vs Netflix US ~$16). ARPU expansion comes from: (a) reducing free tiers, (b) premium content gating, (c) ad-supported tiers at scale, (d) bundling (telecom+OTT). Rising ARPU is the single most important driver of unit economics.
Hours viewed per ₹1Cr content spend; >5M streaming hours

Raw content spend is meaningless without measuring what it produces. Hours viewed per crore of content investment measures efficiency. A ₹100 Cr movie that generates 500M hours (like Jawan on Netflix) is far better value than a ₹50 Cr series watched by 5M people. Library content efficiency (old catalog generating views) is the ultimate competitive advantage.

MAU measures platform reach; DAU/MAU measures engagement quality. A platform with high MAU but low DAU/MAU has a sampling problem — users install but don't return. >30% DAU/MAU signals habitual usage. Engagement drives both ad revenue (more impressions per user) and subscription retention.

Subscriber churn is the silent killer of OTT economics. India's low ARPU means customer acquisition cost recovery takes 6-12 months — if a subscriber churns within 3 months, the company lost money on them. Churn spikes after tentpole events (users subscribe for IPL then cancel). Low churn signals content stickiness.
EBITDA breakeven within 3 years; cash burn declining QoQ

Most Indian OTT platforms are loss-making. The key question is the trajectory — is cash burn declining? Is there a credible path to EBITDA breakeven? Compare cash burn vs subscriber growth to assess unit economics. Platforms burning ₹500+ Cr/year with flat subscriber growth are destroying value.

Original content drives subscriber acquisition (people sign up for a specific show). Licensed content drives retention (catalog depth keeps them engaged between originals). The ideal mix is 40%+ originals for marketing/acquisition value, with a deep licensed library for daily engagement.
Print ad revenue stable or declining <5% YoY

Print advertising is in structural decline globally, but India's regional print market has been resilient. English dailies (HT, TOI) face steeper digital migration of ad budgets. Regional language dailies (Dainik Bhaskar, Eenadu) retain better because local advertisers have fewer digital alternatives. Monitoring the rate of decline — not whether it's declining — is what matters.

India is unique — print readership has historically grown even as other countries declined, driven by rising literacy and regional language demand. IRS (Indian Readership Survey) data shows total readership. Companies maintaining circulation in their core geography have defensible ad revenue.
Digital revenue >20% of total; growing >30% YoY

The survival metric for print media. Companies that successfully build digital properties (Times Internet, HT Digital) capture migrating ad budgets and add subscription revenue. Digital revenue mix >20% signals successful transition; <10% means the company is still mostly analog and vulnerable.
Newsprint cost <30% of revenue; hedged/contracted pricing

Newsprint is 25-35% of print media cost, entirely imported (India imports ~60% of newsprint). Crude-linked (energy for pulp manufacturing) and forex-sensitive. Companies with long-term contracts, domestic sourcing, or integrated paper mills (HT Media's partnership with BILT) are better insulated. Rising newsprint costs directly compress margins.

Print media margins have compressed from 25-30% (pre-2015) to 15-20% due to declining ad revenue and rising newsprint costs. Companies maintaining >18% margins have successfully cut costs and grown digital revenue. Margin expansion signals successful digital transition.

Print media assets include printing presses, real estate (office/press sites), and inventory. ROCE >12% means capital is earning above cost of equity despite the structural headwinds. Companies sitting on valuable real estate (Mumbai/Delhi press sites) may have hidden asset value.

India's box office is ~₹12,000 Cr annually (post-COVID recovery). Revenue is driven by: (a) ticket pricing power (ATP — average ticket price), (b) footfall/occupancy, (c) number of screens. Hindi box office has become top-heavy (₹500Cr+ blockbusters vs mid-range decline), while South Indian cinema has surged (RRR, KGF, Pushpa crossing ₹1,000 Cr).

PVR INOX (India's largest chain, ~1,700 screens) operates at 25-35% average occupancy. Each 1% occupancy improvement drops straight to EBITDA. ATP (Average Ticket Price) at ₹250+ in metros and ₹150+ in Tier 2/3 ensures per-head revenue covers the high fixed cost of screen operations. F&B spend per head (₹100-150) is equally important — it's 25-30% of revenue at 70%+ gross margin.

India has ~9,500 multiplex screens vs ~60,000 in China and ~45,000 in the US. Screens per million population is 7 in India vs 40+ in developed markets. Massive expansion headroom, especially in Tier 2/3 cities. PVR INOX targets 150+ screens/year. New screens take 18-24 months to mature to full occupancy.
≥2 hits per quarter; diversified language portfolio

Film production is inherently hit-driven — 10% of films generate 80% of profits. Companies with diversified language exposure (Hindi + South + regional) reduce concentration risk. A studio releasing 15-20 films/year with a 30%+ hit ratio has a more predictable revenue stream than one betting on 3-4 big-budget films.
SPH (spend per head) ≥₹120; F&B revenue >25% of total

Food & beverage is the secret profit engine of exhibition — it's 25-30% of revenue but 35-40% of EBITDA because gross margins are 70-75% (vs 45-50% on tickets). Increasing F&B SPH through menu innovation, premium formats (IMAX, 4DX), and dine-in concepts directly improves profitability without needing more footfall.

Exhibition is capital-intensive (each new screen costs ₹3-5 Cr) and lease-heavy (long-term mall/property leases). PVR INOX merger created ₹3,000+ Cr of lease obligations. Rising interest rates and fixed lease costs make operating leverage work in reverse during low-occupancy quarters. Debt serviceability is critical.

Film exhibition earns returns on screen assets, leasehold improvements, and projection equipment. ROCE >12% signals screens are earning above cost of capital. New screens depress ROCE initially (18-24 month maturity). Mature screen portfolio should target >15%.
Digital ad revenue growing >25% YoY; >50% of billings from digital

India's advertising market is ~₹1L Cr, growing at 10-12% CAGR overall but digital is growing at 25-30%. Agencies that have pivoted to digital (programmatic, social, influencer) are capturing this shift. Those still dependent on traditional media buying (TV, print) face margin pressure as media buying becomes automated/disintermediated.
Top 10 clients <40% of revenue; client churn <10%

Agency revenue is project/retainer based — losing a major client can crater revenue overnight. Low client concentration and high retention signal service quality and relationship depth. Diversified client base across sectors (FMCG, auto, tech, BFSI) also reduces cyclical risk.
≥18% for integrated agencies; ≥12% for media buying

Advertising is a people business with high operating leverage — once the team is staffed, incremental revenue flows largely to EBITDA. Global benchmarks: WPP ~15%, Publicis ~18%. Indian agencies should target 15-20%. Below 12% signals over-hiring or price competition.

Agencies sell human creativity and expertise. Revenue per employee measures productivity and pricing power. AI/automation tools (programmatic ad buying, generative AI for creative) are raising productivity — agencies adopting these early will see rising revenue per employee without proportional headcount growth.
DSO <90 days; net working capital days <30

Agencies face chronic working capital strain — they pay media vendors upfront but collect from clients with 60-120 day credit cycles. Managing this gap is essential for cash flow. Companies with strong collection practices and media vendor terms generate positive operating cash flow.
Advertising is asset-light (office space, computers, talent). ROCE should be >20% for well-run agencies. Low ROCE signals either poor pricing, over-staffing, or locked working capital.
≥25% YoY; real-money gaming (RMG) + esports both growing

India's gaming market is ~$3.5B and growing at 20-25% CAGR. Real-money gaming (fantasy sports, poker, rummy) is the primary revenue driver — Nazara Technologies, Games24x7, Dream11. Revenue growth must come from both user acquisition and ARPU improvement, not just player volume. Regulatory risk (state-level bans on RMG) can cause sudden revenue drops.

For gaming platforms, the key metric is not downloads or MAU but Monthly Transacting Users — people who actually spend money. MTU/MAU conversion rate measures monetization efficiency. India has 500M+ mobile gamers but <50M transact. Converting free players to paying customers (through in-app purchases, battle passes, tournament entry fees) is the growth lever.

Indian gaming ARPU is low globally (~₹200-500/month for RMG) but growing. ARPU expansion comes from: (a) higher-stakes games, (b) in-app purchase depth, (c) subscription models, (d) esports tournament entry fees. Companies that can move ARPU from ₹200 → ₹500 while maintaining user base double their revenue.

Gaming companies spend heavily on user acquisition (performance marketing, influencer campaigns, sports sponsorship). If Lifetime Value (LTV) is not >3x Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC), the economics don't work. India's CAC for gaming users has risen 40-50% since 2022 due to competition and regulatory restrictions on advertising RMG.
Operating in regulated states; no exposure to banned formats

India's gaming regulation is fragmented — some states ban online RMG (Tamil Nadu attempted, overturned), others tax it at 28% GST (significantly impacting unit economics). Companies operating purely in skill-based games with regulatory clarity (rummy, fantasy sports post-Supreme Court rulings) have lower risk. Those in grey areas (casual betting, prediction markets) face existential regulatory risk.

Gaming platforms earn a take rate (commission) on each transaction — typically 10-20% for RMG. Higher take rate = better economics but risks driving users to competitors. Gross margin >60% signals efficient platform operations. 28% GST on full face value (not just platform fees) has compressed margins for RMG platforms since Oct 2023.

Most Indian gaming companies are in growth/investment phase. EBITDA positivity signals the platform has crossed scale threshold. Nazara Technologies achieved EBITDA breakeven in FY24. Margin expansion from here requires operating leverage — growing revenue faster than marketing/tech spend.
Pre-sales >₹5,000 Cr/year for large developers; ≥20% YoY growth

Pre-sales (value of new bookings) is THE leading indicator for real estate companies — it predicts revenue 2-3 years ahead because revenue recognition under Ind AS 115 happens at completion/possession. Godrej Properties, Prestige, DLF report pre-sales quarterly. Pre-sales growth reflects demand strength, pricing power, and launch success. Declining pre-sales in a rising market signals competitive weakness.

Collections are actual cash received from customers. Pre-sales without collections is just an agreement — the cash hasn't arrived. Collections/pre-sales ratio >75% means buyers are making payments on schedule (linked to construction progress milestones). Low collections ratio signals either construction delays (RERA deadlines not met) or buyer defaults. Collections fund construction — without them, the developer needs external debt.

The real estate-specific leverage metric. Unlike D/E (which ignores business strength), Net Debt/Pre-sales measures deleveraging capacity from current business momentum. <0.5x means the developer can repay all debt in 6 months from current pre-sales trajectory. Godrej Properties maintains ~0.3x. Companies with >1.0x are over-leveraged relative to sales velocity. This metric predicted every major Indian real estate default (Unitech, JP Group, Amrapali).
Launch pipeline >2x annual pre-sales; diversified across micro-markets

Launch pipeline is the land bank converted to RERA-approved projects ready for launch. It determines growth runway — developers without adequate launch pipeline face pre-sales stagnation. Post-RERA, land acquisition + approvals takes 2-3 years before a project can be launched. Companies with 5+ year pipelines (DLF, Prestige, Godrej Properties) have longer growth visibility. Pipeline should be diversified across cities/micro-markets to avoid geographic concentration risk.

Real estate development margins vary by segment: luxury >35%, mid-segment 25-30%, affordable 15-20%. JV/DM (joint development/asset-light) models have higher EBITDA margins because land cost is minimal. REITs/rental income businesses operate at >60% EBITDA margins because operating costs are low relative to rental income. Margin trend matters — improving margins in a rising ASP (average selling price) environment signals pricing power.

Real estate is capital-intensive — massive capital is locked in land, construction WIP, and unsold inventory. ROCE measures if the capital deployed is earning above cost. Asset-light developers (Godrej Properties uses JV/DM model) achieve higher ROCE than land-heavy ones. ROCE improvement signals the developer is accelerating project execution and reducing capital lock-in periods.

The #1 metric. Pre-sales = new home bookings by value. Growth should come from both volume (units sold) and ASP (average selling price per sq ft) improvement. Pure ASP-driven growth without volume means pricing has outrun demand. Volume-driven growth with flat/declining ASP means the developer is discounting to sell. Best: both volume and ASP rising.

Cash from buyers funds construction. Collections/pre-sales >75% means current-year buyers are paying on schedule AND older project buyers are making milestone payments. Collections exceeding pre-sales (ratio >100%) means the developer is collecting from a large backlog of sold-but-under-construction units — strong cash position.
Unsold inventory <18 months (at current quarterly run rate)

Unsold inventory = completed but not sold units. Measured in months of absorption. <12 months is excellent (DLF, Prestige). 12-18 months is healthy. 18-36 months signals sluggish demand. >36 months is distressed inventory. Unsold inventory trend matters — declining inventory in a rising price environment is very bullish.

Post-2018 (IL&FS crisis, NBFC collapse), over-leveraged developers (Unitech, Jaypee, Supertech) went bankrupt. The market now rewards low-leverage developers. Net D/E <0.5x provides buffer for execution delays and demand slowdowns. Godrej Properties ~0.3x, Prestige ~0.4x post-restructuring.
Average delivery within RERA timeline; <30 months for mid-rise

RERA mandates project completion within registered timeline (typically 3-5 years). Developers who consistently deliver on time build brand equity and earn customer trust (which drives referral sales, 30-40% of bookings for top developers). Delayed projects incur RERA penalties and erode brand.
Presence in ≥3 major markets; no single city >50% of pre-sales

Real estate is hyperlocal — a slowdown in one city (e.g., Gurugram oversupply 2016-2019) can devastate a developer. Companies present across Mumbai + Bangalore + Pune + NCR + Hyderabad have diversified demand sources. But new city entry is capital-intensive and risky (execution track record doesn't transfer easily).

Residential development locks capital in land and WIP for 3-5 years. ROCE >12% means project IRRs are healthy. Asset-light models (JV with landowner, development management) improve ROCE because the developer deploys minimal capital. ROCE improvement over time signals faster execution and efficient capital deployment.

Occupancy rate is the most important metric for commercial real estate — it directly drives rental income. India's Grade A office market averages 85-90% occupancy. Top developers (DLF Cyber City, Embassy, Brookfield) maintain >90%. Pre-leasing (signing tenants before construction completion) de-risks new supply.

Rental yield = annual rental income / property value. India office yields are 7-9% (higher than global 4-6%). Net Operating Income (NOI) margin = (rental income - property operating expenses) / rental income. NOI margin >70% is standard for Grade A office because operating costs (maintenance, property tax, insurance) are a small fraction of rent.

Office leases are typically 5-9 years with built-in annual rent escalations (5-15% depending on market). Mark-to-market gap measures how much current rents are below market rates — a high gap means rents will jump when leases renew. Embassy REIT reported 20-25% MTM gap, implying significant rental growth locked in.
WALE >5 years; >70% of rental from MNC/blue-chip tenants

Weighted Average Lease Expiry (WALE) >5 years provides multi-year rental visibility. Blue-chip tenants (top IT companies, MNCs, Big 4) have near-zero default risk. A building with WALE of 3 years and mostly startup tenants is riskier than one with WALE of 7 years and Fortune 500 tenants.
New completions >1M sq ft/year; development pipeline >5x annual completion

Portfolio growth comes from new completions and acquisitions. A robust development pipeline provides visibility on future rental income growth. Companies with large land banks near existing commercial clusters (DLF in Gurgaon, Embassy in Bangalore outer ring road) have embedded growth optionality.
Net Debt / Annual Rental Income <5x; debt cost < rental yield

Commercial real estate is typically debt-funded. The key test: is the rental yield (8-9%) higher than the cost of debt (8-10%)? If yes, leverage amplifies returns. Net Debt / Annual Rental Income <5x means the portfolio generates enough rental to service and repay debt within reasonable timeframe.
≥8% (lower than residential due to annuity model)

Commercial RE locks capital for decades (annuity model) but generates predictable returns. ROCE of 8-10% is acceptable given the stability and predictability of cash flows. NAV-based valuation (price vs net asset value) is more relevant than ROCE for commercial portfolios.

Top Indian malls (Phoenix Palladium, DLF Promenade, Forum Malls) operate at 95-98% occupancy. Lower occupancy means empty storefronts, which reduces foot traffic and hurts adjacent stores (negative spiral). Wait-listed tenants indicate excess demand — the mall can be selective about tenant mix and negotiate higher rents.
Revenue per Sq Ft (Rental + Revenue Share)

Mall revenue comes from: (a) minimum guarantee rent (MG), (b) revenue share above MG (typically 8-15% of tenant sales), (c) CAM charges. Revenue per sq ft captures total monetization. Phoenix Mills earns ₹150-250/sq ft/month across its portfolio. Revenue share component is key — it aligns the mall's income with tenant success.

The ultimate health metric for a mall — are shoppers spending more? Same-mall consumption growth (total tenant sales within existing malls) drives both revenue share income and lease renewal rates. Post-COVID, India's premium malls have seen 20-30% consumption growth as discretionary spending surges.
Footfall growing >10% YoY; trading density (sales/footfall) rising

More visitors = more sales = more revenue for both tenants and the mall. But quality of footfall matters — trading density (average spend per visitor) should be rising. Entertainment anchors (multiplexes, family entertainment centers) drive footfall. Premium F&B and luxury brands drive trading density.

Optimal tenant mix creates a destination, not just a shopping center. Over-reliance on any category is risky. Low churn (<10%) means tenants are profitable and want to stay. The ability to attract aspirational brands (Zara, H&M, Apple) signals mall positioning.
Net Debt/EBITDA <4x; declining with rental growth

Mall development requires heavy upfront investment (₹500-2,000 Cr per mall). Debt-funded growth is normal but must be manageable. Net Debt/EBITDA <4x means the mall portfolio generates enough to comfortably service debt. Phoenix Mills has brought leverage down to ~2x through strong rental growth.
≥10% for mature malls; improving for recently opened

Mall ROCE improves over time as occupancy rises and rents escalate. Newly opened malls (first 2-3 years) have lower ROCE due to tenant fit-out periods and gradual ramp-up. Mature malls (5+ years) should achieve >10% ROCE with further expansion through rent escalation.

REITs are legally required to distribute ≥90% of net distributable income to unitholders. DPU (₹ per unit) is the primary return metric — it's the REIT equivalent of dividends. DPU yield (DPU/unit price) should be compared against fixed deposit rates and bond yields. Embassy REIT yields ~6-7%, Mindspace ~6.5%. Rising DPU signals portfolio growth and rent escalation.

Occupancy drives rental income which drives DPU. India's listed REITs (Embassy Office Parks, Mindspace, Brookfield India) operate at 85-92% occupancy. Re-leasing spread (% increase in rent when a lease is renewed) measures pricing power. A 15% re-leasing spread means rents jump 15% at renewal — pure margin expansion.
Net Asset Value (NAV) vs Market Price
Trading at <10% premium to NAV; or discount to NAV for value

NAV = appraised value of all properties minus debt. REITs trading at a discount to NAV offer value because you're buying real estate below its independently appraised worth. Premium to NAV is justified if the REIT has a strong development pipeline that will add value. India REITs have traded at 5-15% discount to NAV — attractive vs global REITs.
WALE >5 years; no single year with >20% of leases expiring

WALE tells you how long current rental income is contractually locked in. >5 years provides excellent visibility. Concentrated lease expiry (e.g., 30% of leases expiring in one year) creates risk — if market conditions are weak, renewals may be at lower rents.
Debt/GAV <35%; weighted average cost of debt <8.5%

SEBI mandates REIT leverage cap at 49% of GAV. Prudent REITs maintain 25-35%. Lower leverage means less interest cost eating into distributable income. Cost of debt below rental yield (~8-9%) means debt is accretive — each borrowed rupee generates more rental income than interest cost.
Development pipeline >20% of current leasable area

REITs grow DPU through: (a) rent escalation on existing portfolio, (b) completing new developments, (c) acquiring new properties. A strong development pipeline (under-construction office parks that will be added to the REIT) provides visible growth without acquisition risk.
≥70% rental from Fortune 500/top IT; sector diversification

Blue-chip tenants (Google, Microsoft, JP Morgan, TCS) have near-zero default risk and long lease commitments. Concentration in one tenant sector (e.g., >80% from IT/tech) creates sector-specific risk. India REITs are heavily IT-tenant concentrated — diversification toward BFSI, GCCs, and flex space is positive.
Warehouse additions >5M sq ft/year; pipeline >20M sq ft

India's Grade A warehousing market has exploded post-GST (single national market) and e-commerce boom. Players: IndoSpace (largest, unlisted), ESR, Welspun One, Lodha Industrial. Leasable area growth drives rental income growth. India has ~300M sq ft of Grade A warehouse stock vs >15B sq ft in the US — massive underpenetration.

Warehouse occupancy >90% signals strong demand. India's Grade A warehousing operates at 85-92% occupancy across major clusters (Mumbai, NCR, Bangalore, Pune, Chennai). Pre-leasing (tenants commit before construction completion) de-risks development. E-commerce and 3PL tenants (Amazon, Flipkart, Delhivery) sign long-term leases.

Warehouse rental yields in India (8-10%) are higher than office (7-9%) because of lower construction cost per sq ft. Lease escalation clauses of 4-5% per year provide built-in rental growth. The delta between yield and cost of debt determines leverage economics.

E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart, Meesho) and 3PL logistics (Delhivery, Mahindra Logistics) are the primary tenants. Manufacturing tenants (auto, FMCG) provide diversification. High-quality tenants with long leases (5-15 years for warehouse) provide cash flow stability.
Near major consumption centers / ports / highways; GST regime-compliant

Warehouse location is everything — proximity to consumption centers (Mumbai, NCR), ports (JNPT, Mundra), and highway intersections determines tenant demand and rental rates. GST eliminated multi-state warehousing need, centralizing demand in fewer, larger facilities near key logistics nodes.

Warehouses have simpler operating requirements than offices — lower maintenance, no complex HVAC, minimal common area expenses. NOI margins of 75-80% are achievable. Higher spec facilities (cold storage, clean rooms) have slightly lower NOI but command premium rents.

Warehousing development earns returns over long horizons (15-20 year leases). ROCE of 10%+ is healthy given the stability and predictability. Development-stage companies have lower current ROCE but embedded NAV growth from pipeline completion.

RE services include property broking (NoBroker, Housing.com, Square Yards), property management, valuation services, and co-working (WeWork India). Revenue grows with: (a) transaction volume (more property sales = more brokerage), (b) digital platform adoption, (c) value-added services (home loans, interiors). India's RE services market is largely unorganized — formalization is the secular tailwind.
GMV growing >25% YoY; take rate stable at ≥2%

For platforms and brokerages, GMV (total transaction value facilitated) is the scale metric. Take rate (commission as % of property value) is typically 1-3% for residential, 3-5% for commercial. Higher GMV with stable take rate = sustainable revenue growth. NoBroker has disrupted traditional broking by charging flat fees instead of percentage commission.
≥15% for tech-enabled platforms; ≥10% for traditional broking

Tech-enabled RE services (NoBroker, Square Yards) have higher margin potential due to platform leverage. Traditional broking is people-intensive with 5-10% margins. Co-working (WeWork India) has negative margins currently but path to EBITDA breakeven. Property management is steady at 10-15% margins.

RE is a high-value, low-frequency purchase — customer acquisition costs are high (₹5,000-15,000 per qualified lead for broking). Companies with strong SEO/brand recall (Housing.com, MagicBricks) have lower CAC through organic traffic. Digital marketing spend efficiency (cost per lead, lead-to-conversion ratio) is the key operational metric.
Repeat users >30%; tenant management/renewal services active

Property transactions are infrequent (once in 5-10 years for purchase). Companies that build ongoing relationships through rental management, home services (interiors, maintenance), and tenant management create recurring revenue between transactions. NoBroker's subscription model for landlord/tenant management adds stickiness.
Top 3 in target geography; growing share of organized market

India's real estate broking market is 95% unorganized. Organized players capturing share from local brokers through technology (virtual tours, digital documentation, loan facilitation) have a large TAM. Market share growth in organized RE services is a stronger signal than absolute size.
Positive operating cash flow; low capex needs

RE services are asset-light (technology platform, small offices). Working capital should be minimal. Companies burning cash on marketing without proportional revenue growth are destroying value. Break-even to positive cash flow is the minimum expectation for scaled platforms.

Consumer services benefit from rising discretionary spending, urbanization, and formalization. India’s hospitality, QSR, and organized retail markets are growing 15-20% as branded/organized players displace unorganized.
SSSG isolates organic growth from new store/property openings. >8% indicates pricing power and footfall improvement at existing locations. Negative SSSG with aggressive expansion is unsustainable.

Hotels have high operating leverage (50-60% EBITDA at peak). QSR chains target 18-25%. Retail operates on thin margins (6-10%) but high asset turnover. Margin profile varies dramatically by sub-sector.

Fixed-cost businesses need high utilization. Hotels above 70% occupancy see profit margins expand rapidly. Retail revenue per sq ft measures store productivity. Restaurants track covers per table per day.
Store-level EBITDA positive by Month 6; payback <3 years

For expansion-driven models (QSR, retail chains), unit economics determine whether growth creates or destroys value. Store-level profitability within 6 months and payback within 3 years is the benchmark.

Hotels are asset-heavy (12-15% ROCE typical). Restaurant chains using franchise/management models are asset-light (25%+). Retail ROCE depends on inventory management. Asset-light models command valuation premium.
RevPAR = Occupancy × ADR. The single most important hotel metric combining pricing and utilization. Indian Hotels (Taj) RevPAR ~₹8,000+. RevPAR growth >10% indicates both demand and pricing strength.

Hotels have massive operating leverage — incremental revenue above 65% occupancy flows to profit at 70-80% margin. India’s branded hotels average 68-72% occupancy. New supply is limited, supporting rate recovery.

Owned hotels earn 25-35% EBITDA (fixed costs: staff, maintenance). Management/franchise model earns 50-70% margin on fee income. Indian Hotels transitioning to asset-light (Ginger brand) for higher margins.

Asset-light expansion (management contracts, franchising) generates high-margin recurring fees without capital lock-in. ITC Hotels, Lemon Tree growing through management contracts. Higher asset-light mix = higher valuation multiple.

India has ~150,000 branded hotel rooms (vs. 5M+ in US). Huge headroom for branded chain expansion. Pipeline of signed rooms indicates growth visibility. Indian Hotels targets 300+ hotels (currently ~250+).

Hotels are extremely capital-intensive (₹1-2 Cr per room for upscale). ROCE >12% is healthy for owned properties. Asset-light models achieve 25%+ ROCE. Blended ROCE improving with asset-light mix shift.
SSSG measures organic growth at existing restaurants. >8% indicates pricing power and customer traffic improvement. Negative SSSG with new store openings masks deteriorating brand strength.

Store-level EBITDA isolates operational efficiency from corporate overhead. Jubilant FoodWorks (Domino’s) ~22%. Devyani (KFC/Pizza Hut) ~20%. Below 15% at store level means the unit economics don’t support expansion.

QSR growth is store-count driven. Jubilant targets 200+ stores/yr. Devyani 300+/yr. Net store additions (new minus closures) reveal expansion quality. High closures alongside openings signals poor site selection.
ARS declining with store additions means cannibalization or lower-tier city expansion diluting averages. Stable/rising ARS with store growth confirms scalable economics.

Food cost is the largest variable expense. Supply chain efficiency, menu engineering, and procurement scale drive this down. Companies with central kitchens and standardized menus achieve better food cost ratios.

Restaurants are moderately capital-intensive (₹50L-2Cr per outlet for QSR). ROCE >20% is achievable for mature QSR chains. Asset-light franchise models earn higher ROCE. <15% means the expansion pace doesn’t justify capital deployed.

India’s organized retail is ~10% of total retail market ($950B). DMart, Reliance Retail, Trent are growing 20-30% through store additions and same-store growth. Massive unorganized-to-organized shift underway.
SSSG isolates organic growth from new store openings. DMart achieves 8-12% SSSG consistently. Negative SSSG indicates traffic decline, competitive pressure, or cannibalization from own new stores.
Store productivity metric. DMart ~₹35,000/sq ft (industry-leading). Shoppers Stop ~₹12,000. Higher rev/sq ft indicates better merchandise selection, pricing, and foot traffic conversion.

Grocery retail (DMart) operates on 15% gross margin but high inventory turns. Fashion retail (Trent, Shoppers Stop) earns 45-55% gross margin but lower turns. Business model determines margin vs. turnover trade-off.
Inventory turns measure working capital efficiency. DMart ~12-14x (fresh grocery moves fast). Fashion retailers ~3-5x (seasonal collections). High turns compensate for thin margins in value retail.

Retail ROCE depends on the owned vs. leased model. DMart (owned stores) ~20%. Leased stores show higher ROCE on lower capital but face rental escalation risk. Private label mix improvement drives ROCE expansion.
Operating margin varies sharply — upstream (E&P) benefits from high crude but downstream (refining/marketing) gets squeezed. ≥12% upstream, ≥5% downstream is healthy.
Earnings volatility driver. Companies with hedging/subsidy pass-through (IOCs) have lower sensitivity. High sensitivity = uncontrollable earnings swings.
Refiners book inventory gains/losses as crude prices swing. Excluding this shows core operating performance. Persistent losses signal poor inventory management.
Gross Refining Margin is the profitability per barrel refined. Singapore complex GRM benchmark $6-8/bbl; Indian refiners (RIL, BPCL) target $8-12 via complexity advantage.
Capex-heavy sector — high leverage amplifies commodity cycle pain. Debt/Equity <0.8x gives flexibility to invest through downturns without distress.
Cash flow confirms earnings aren't just accounting gains (inventory revaluation). CFO/EBITDA >70% shows real cash generation from operations.
Measures whether capex is funded from internal cash flow. <60% means growth is self-funded; >80% risks overleveraging during price downturns.
RRR >100% means the company discovers/acquires more oil than it produces, ensuring long-term production sustainability. ONGC’s RRR has been declining — key risk.
All-in cost of extracting one barrel. ONGC ~$8-10/bbl, global majors $10-15. Lower lifting cost = profitable at lower crude prices. >$20 = vulnerable in downcycles.
Revenue growth in E&P is volume-driven. India’s domestic production is stagnant (~30 MMT crude/yr). Companies growing production >5% YoY are gaining through new discoveries or EOR.
Indian E&P companies (ONGC, Oil India) sell at a discount to Brent due to Govt subsidy sharing. Realization closer to Brent = less Govt intervention in pricing.
E&P is inherently risky (exploration failures, dry wells). Low leverage (<0.5x) ensures survival through failed exploration campaigns and crude price crashes.
Under-investing in exploration leads to reserve depletion. ≥30% of capex on exploration ensures future discoveries while development capex sustains current production.
E&P companies enjoy high margins when crude is above breakeven. ONGC ~40-50% EBITDA margin. Below 30% signals excessive subsidy burden or high operating costs.
GRM = revenue per barrel refined minus crude cost. Singapore benchmark $5-7; complex Indian refiners (RIL $8-12, BPCL $7-9) earn premium via Nelson Complexity.
Refining is a fixed-cost business. >95% utilization maximizes per-barrel cost absorption. Indian refineries run at 100-110% (de-bottlenecked capacity). <90% wastes fixed costs.
Marketing margin on petrol/diesel is the retail profit. Govt-controlled pricing impacts IOC/BPCL/HPCL. ₹3/L is healthy; <₹1/L means under-recoveries eroding refining gains.
Crude price swings create inventory gains/losses that distort reported margins. Always look at core GRM excluding inventory effect for true refining profitability.
Higher complexity = ability to process cheaper heavy/sour crude into high-value products. RIL Jamnagar NCI ~14 (world’s most complex). Higher NCI = higher GRM premium.
Refiners need capex for capacity expansion and maintenance shutdowns. Debt/Equity <0.8x is manageable; >1.2x strains interest coverage during low-GRM periods.
Retail fuel outlets are last-mile distribution. IOC >34,000, BPCL ~21,000. Wider network = more volume throughput + non-fuel revenue (convenience stores, EV charging).
Gas transmission/distribution is a volume game. India’s gas share in energy mix is ~6% (target 15%). GAIL transmits ~120 MMSCMD. Volume growth = revenue growth.
Pipeline infrastructure is the moat. Utilization >75% ensures fixed cost absorption. India has ~22,000 km gas pipeline with expansion to ~35,000 km planned.
Gas transmission earns regulated tariffs (high-margin). Gas marketing/trading is low-margin but high-volume. GAIL’s transmission EBITDA >60%; overall blended ~15-20%.
Long-term gas supply contracts (LNG import, domestic allocation) provide volume certainty. Spot gas is volatile. >70% contracted = predictable revenue.
Pipeline capex is heavy (₹5-7 Cr/km). Manageable debt ensures expansion capacity. GAIL ~0.3x is conservative; Petronet LNG ~0.4x.
PNGRB sets transmission tariffs ensuring 12-14% post-tax return on equity. Favorable regulation = predictable earnings. Unfavorable tariff orders squeeze pipeline returns.
Gas infrastructure is capital-heavy. ROCE >12% is healthy given the regulated nature. Below 10% means the regulated tariff isn’t generating adequate returns on invested capital.
India produces ~1,000 MT coal/yr (Coal India ~700 MT). Govt target 1,500 MT by 2030. Companies growing production >8% are gaining allocation and operational efficiency.
Coal sold via e-auction earns market-linked premiums above Govt-notified price. Higher e-auction share = better realizations. Coal India e-auction ~15-20% of sales at 50-100% premium.
Pit-head cost determines profitability. Coal India avg ~₹1,500-1,800/ton. OBR (overburden ratio), mechanization, and mine quality drive cost position.
Coal mining has high operating leverage. EBITDA margin >25% is healthy (Coal India ~28-32% in good years). Margin compresses when Govt restricts price hikes.
Coal piles up at pithead if dispatch infrastructure (railways, roads) is inadequate. Offtake ratio >90% means supply chain works. <80% = logistics bottleneck destroying value.
Coal companies are mature, cash-generative businesses with limited reinvestment needs. Payout >50% returns cash to shareholders. Coal India’s dividend yield is 6-8%.
Cracker spread (product price minus feedstock) is the core margin metric. Spread >$300/ton is profitable; below $200 = cash-burn territory. Cyclical — peaks and troughs matter.
Ability to switch between naphtha, ethane, propane based on which is cheapest. RIL’s dual-feed cracker gives $50-100/ton cost advantage vs. single-feed competitors.
Petrochemical plants have high fixed costs. >90% utilization ensures fixed cost absorption. RIL’s petchem complex runs at 95%+ utilization.
Integrated R2P (refinery-to-petrochemicals) captures value across the chain. RIL converts 15%+ of crude to petchem. Standalone crackers are more vulnerable to cycle swings.
Petchem margins are cyclical (10-25%). >15% through-cycle signals cost advantage. India’s per-capita polymer consumption is 11 kg vs. 40 kg global avg — structural demand growth.
Moving from commodity polymers (PE, PP) to specialty chemicals (adhesives, coatings, surfactants) adds margin stability and reduces commodity cycle dependence.
Volume growth (not value) shows real demand. Indian FMCG leaders (HUL, Nestlé) target 8-12% volume growth; <5% signals category saturation or market share loss.
Brand power shows up in gross margin. FMCG leaders maintain >50% (HUL ~57%, Nestlé ~55%). <45% means weak pricing power or commodity exposure.
Asset-light business should generate high returns. Nifty FMCG cos. average 30-50% ROCE; <25% suggests poor capital allocation or value-destructive acquisitions.
Moat metric — FMCG is a distribution game. Wider reach = higher volumes per SKU = operating leverage. HUL reaches ~9M outlets; depth matters as much as breadth.
Ability to pass on raw material inflation without losing volume. True pricing power = price hikes > CPI without demand destruction. Key in India's inflationary cycles.
Raw material (palm oil, milk, wheat) volatility directly hits margins. Best FMCG firms keep input costs <40% of sales through backward integration and hedging.
Low-capex model should convert nearly all profit to cash. FCF/PAT >90% confirms earnings quality; <70% flags working capital inefficiency or over-investment.
True demand indicator stripped of price hikes. Nestlé India ~8-10%, Britannia ~5-8%. India’s packaged food penetration ~10% vs. 60% globally — massive headroom.
Reflects brand pricing power vs. raw material costs (wheat, milk, edible oil). Nestlé ~57%, Britannia ~40%. <45% signals commodity exposure or weak brand premium.
Category leadership drives shelf space, distribution priority, and advertising efficiency. Market leaders in 2+ categories have compounding advantages.
FMCG is a distribution game. Wider direct distribution = more consistent availability = higher volumes. Nestlé ~5M outlets; ITC ~6M+.
Asset-light manufacturing with high brand value should deliver >30% ROCE. Nestlé ~80%, Britannia ~40%. Below 25% signals poor capital allocation.
≥5% of revenue from launches <3 yrs old
Innovation drives category expansion and premiumization. 5%+ revenue from recent launches shows the company is refreshing its portfolio, not just milking old brands.
Packaged foods require moderate capex (factories, cold chain). FCF/PAT >85% confirms earnings translate to cash. <70% flags overinvestment or working capital issues.
Household products (detergents, cleaners) are staples with steady demand. Volume growth 5-10% indicates real demand in a mature, high-penetration category.
Palm oil, LAB (surfactants) are key inputs. Gross margin >48% indicates brand premium absorbing commodity volatility. HUL home care ~48-52%.
Household products are duopoly/oligopoly markets (HUL + P&G + Godrej dominate). #1/#2 position = shelf space advantage and negotiation leverage with retailers.
Ability to raise prices without losing volume share. In inflationary periods, firms with pricing power protect gross margins; others resort to grammage cuts.
Household products have low capex needs and established brands. ROCE >35% is expected. HUL home care >50%. <30% suggests excessive ad spend or poor product mix.
India’s rural market is the growth frontier for FMCG. Companies with >35% rural revenue benefit from rising rural incomes and sachets/small-pack strategy.
Personal care is premiumizing rapidly in India. Revenue growth 10-15% reflects both volume gains and mix improvement. Marico ~10-12%, Dabur ~8-10%.
Personal care carries highest gross margins in FMCG (brand premium on skincare, haircare). >55% signals strong brand equity. HUL beauty ~60%, Colgate ~67%.
Multi-brand portfolio reduces single-brand risk. Companies with 3+ strong brands across segments (hair, skin, oral) are more resilient to category-specific disruption.
India’s personal care market is shifting to premium/natural products. >25% revenue from premium SKUs signals successful trade-up strategy and higher margins.
D2C channels offer higher margins (no distributor/retailer margin) and direct consumer data. >10% e-commerce shows digital adoption. Mamaearth, Nykaa are D2C-native.
Personal care is asset-light with high margins. ROCE >30% is standard. Colgate ~75%, Marico ~35%. Below 25% signals acquisition-related goodwill drag or poor capex decisions.
Tobacco volumes are structurally declining (regulation + awareness). Volume decline <2% is manageable as price hikes offset. ITC cigarette volumes flat to -2% but revenue grows 6-10%.
Tobacco is a monopolistic/duopolistic market with extreme pricing power. ITC cigarettes ~65% EBITDA margin. >35% overall is strong; pricing power compensates for volume decline.
Every budget is a risk event — steep excise hikes (>10%) push consumers to illicit market (28% of cigarettes are illicit). Moderate <5% hikes are absorbable via pricing.
ITC’s strategy to de-risk from tobacco via FMCG, hotels, agri, paperboard. Non-tobacco EBITDA >30% reduces regulatory concentration risk and improves ESG positioning.
Tobacco businesses generate enormous free cash flow due to minimal capex needs. FCF yield >4% provides shareholder returns via dividends/buybacks.
Limited growth reinvestment needed in a declining-volume business. Payout >60% signals capital discipline. ITC’s payout ~55-60% with ~3.5% dividend yield.
India’s food processing industry growing ~15% annually. Govt push (PLI scheme, mega food parks) supports structural growth. Companies at 12-18% are capturing this opportunity.
Food processing operates on thin margins (agri input costs, perishability). EBITDA >12% is healthy. Value-added products (spices, snacks) earn 15-20%; commodity processing <8%.
Raw material (agri commodities) volatility is the #1 margin risk. <55% RM cost signals backward integration or hedging. >65% means commodity-exposed with no pricing buffer.
Moving up the value chain (branded spices vs. raw, ready-to-eat vs. frozen) adds margin. >50% value-added = defensible margins. Tata Consumer ~60%+ branded portfolio.
Food processing requires unbroken cold chain (farm to shelf). Companies with own/controlled cold chain reduce wastage (India wastes ~40% of fresh produce) and ensure quality.
Food processing is moderately capital-intensive (plants, cold storage). ROCE >18% is healthy. <12% suggests overinvestment in capacity without corresponding utilization.
India’s non-alcoholic beverage market growing ~15% CAGR. Low per-capita consumption (vs. global) = long runway. Varun Beverages, Tata Consumer are key plays.
Beverages enjoy high gross margins (water is the main ingredient). Varun Beverages ~55%, United Spirits ~45%. <45% suggests commoditized portfolio or high input costs.
Volume growth strips out pricing effects and shows real demand expansion. Varun Beverages volumes growing 15-20% driven by geographic expansion and new SKUs.
Beverage sales are impulse-driven. Cooler/chiller availability at point of sale directly drives conversion. Wider distribution + more coolers = more impulse purchases.
Scale-driven business with operating leverage. EBITDA >20% indicates efficient bottling and distribution. Varun Beverages ~22-24%. <15% = sub-scale or high distribution costs.
Beverage bottling requires moderate capex (bottling lines, cold chain). ROCE >22% is healthy for the sector. Below 18% means capacity isn’t being utilized efficiently.
Capital is the raw material; ROE >15% signals efficient equity deployment vs. cost of equity (~12-14% in India). Top Nifty Bank cos. average 14-17% ROE.
Core earnings engine — measures spread between lending and borrowing rates. NIM >3% is healthy for Indian banks; NBFCs/HFCs target 2.5-4%.
Survival metric — high NPAs erode capital and require provisions that destroy profits. RBI benchmark: system GNPA ~3%; well-run banks <2%.
Regulatory floor (Basel III ~11.5% incl. buffers). CAR >16% gives room for aggressive credit growth without dilution.
Revenue growth proxy for lenders. India system credit growth ~15-16% YoY; banks growing faster gain market share without quality dilution.
Measures how much of income is consumed by operations. Best-in-class Indian banks <40%; >55% signals bloated cost structure.
CASA provides low-cost stable funding (saves 200-300 bps vs. term deposits). For insurance, >85% persistency means sticky policyholders and lower acquisition costs.

Measures fresh slippages (new NPAs) as a % of opening standard advances. <1% indicates tight underwriting and early warning systems; >2% signals deteriorating asset quality or stress in specific segments.
Banks are equity-leveraged businesses (10-12x leverage). ROE >15% means the bank earns above its cost of equity; top PSBs 12-14%, top private banks 15-18%.
Core spread income. Private banks (HDFC ~4.1%, Kotak ~5.2%) lead; PSBs 2.8-3.2%. Below 2.5% means funding cost disadvantage or aggressive pricing.
Single biggest risk for banks. Every 1% rise in GNPA requires ~60-70 bps of provisions that directly eat into profits. Top private banks <1.5% GNPA.
CASA (Current Account + Savings Account) deposits cost 3-4% vs. term deposits at 6-7%. Higher CASA = lower cost of funds = wider NIM. HDFC ~38%, Kotak ~52%.
RBI minimum 11.5% (incl. buffers). CAR >16% gives headroom for 18-20% credit growth without dilution. Under-capitalized banks must raise equity at unfavorable terms.
Loan book growth drives revenue growth for banks. System credit ~15-16% YoY. Banks growing above system gain market share; <10% means losing relevance.
Operating leverage metric. Best private banks ~38-42%; PSBs 48-55%. Digital adoption (lower branch costs) is key driver. >55% = bloated cost base.
Buffer against future NPA shocks. PCR >70% means the bank has already set aside provisions for most bad loans. <60% = vulnerable to write-off spikes.
Measures profitability of new policies sold. VNB margin >25% means each new policy creates substantial embedded value. SBI Life ~28%, HDFC Life ~26%.
Measures policy renewal rate after 1 year. >85% means sticky customers and lower re-acquisition costs. <80% signals misselling or poor product fit.
Claims + expenses as % of premiums. <100% = underwriting profit. <95% is strong. Indian general insurers average 100-110%; <100% is a competitive advantage.
Annualized Premium Equivalent (life) / Gross Written Premium (general) growth. India's insurance penetration is ~4.2% vs. 7% global avg — large runway supports 15%+ growth.
IRDAI minimum is 150%. Solvency >180% gives buffer for adverse claims and growth without capital raising. <160% constrains new business writing.
Embedded Value = adjusted net worth + value of in-force book. EV growth >15% confirms the business is compounding intrinsic value, not just chasing topline.
Insurance float generates investment income. Higher yield on bond/equity portfolio boosts overall profitability. Life insurers hold longer-duration assets for ALM matching.
Similar to banks but NBFCs operate with higher leverage (4-7x). ROE >15% signals efficient equity deployment. Bajaj Finance ~22%, Shriram ~16%.
NBFCs lack CASA advantage so must earn higher spreads to compensate for wholesale borrowing costs. Retail NBFCs target 5-8%; wholesale 2-4%.
NBFCs lend to underserved segments (higher risk). GNPA <3% is well-managed. Microfinance/unsecured NBFCs may run 3-5% but need higher spreads to compensate.
NBFCs borrow short and lend long — ALM mismatch risk caused IL&FS/DHFL crisis. Positive mismatch (assets maturing before liabilities) in each bucket is critical for survival.
No deposit franchise = market borrowing. AAA-rated NBFCs borrow at 7.5-8.5%; lower-rated at 9-11%. Each 50 bps lower cost = direct NIM pickup.
Assets Under Management growth drives revenue. NBFCs targeting 20%+ AUM growth are gaining market share in their lending segments vs. banks.
Concentration in one funding source (CPs, bonds, bank lines) creates refinancing risk. Diversified borrowing base ensures liquidity even during credit market stress.
RBI mandates 15% for NBFCs. >20% provides growth buffer and signals to lenders that the NBFC is well-capitalized, ensuring lower borrowing costs.
Home loans are low-yield (8.5-9.5%) secured assets. NIM of 2.5-3.5% is healthy. <2% means the HFC is competing purely on rates and destroying value.
Mortgage-backed lending should have low NPAs due to collateral. GNPA <2% is healthy; >3% signals underwriting issues. HDFC Ltd (pre-merger) maintained <1.5%.
New loan disbursements drive AUM growth. India's housing shortage (~10M urban units) supports structural demand. HFCs growing 18%+ gain market share vs. bank home loans.
HFCs operate at 8-12x leverage. ROE >14% is healthy for the low-risk mortgage business. Below 12% means the spread doesn’t justify the capital locked.
HFCs rely on NHB refinance, bonds, and bank lines. Lower cost of funds = wider spread on the same home loan rate. AAA-rated HFCs borrow at 7.5-8%.
Conservative LTV (<75%) means the collateral cushion absorbs price declines. NHB allows up to 90% for small loans but portfolio avg <75% is prudent.
Affordable housing (<₹35L ticket) enjoys Govt push (PMAY subsidy, tax benefits, priority sector lending). Higher mix = structural demand support + regulatory tailwind.
Capital market revenues are linked to market turnover, AUM growth, and IPO activity. 15%+ growth captures structural shift — India’s demat accounts grew from 4Cr to 18Cr+ in 5 years.
Capital market intermediaries are asset-light, high-margin businesses. >35% EBIT margin is healthy; exchanges/rating agencies can exceed 50% due to monopolistic/oligopolistic structure.
Low capital intensity means ROCE should be high. CRISIL >40%, BSE/CDSL >25%. <20% signals poor cost control or undermonetized business segments.
These are winner-take-most businesses with network effects (exchanges) or regulatory barriers (rating agencies, depositories). Market leadership compounds — challengers struggle to dislodge.
SEBI is an active regulator. Penalties, restrictions, or license revocations can permanently impair the business. Clean regulatory record is a prerequisite, not a bonus.
Shift to digital reduces marginal cost to near-zero. Firms with >70% digital transactions enjoy operating leverage — each new user adds revenue with negligible cost.
Asset-light businesses with limited reinvestment needs should return excess cash. Payout >50% signals capital discipline; <30% suggests cash hoarding or poor allocation.
Revenue = AUM × fee rate. AUM growth driven by net inflows + market returns. India MF AUM ~₹65L Cr growing 20%+ annually. SIP flows alone >₹24,000 Cr/month.
Equity AUM earns 60-80 bps fee vs. debt at 15-25 bps. Higher equity mix = higher blended TER. HDFC AMC ~68% equity, Nippon ~52%.
AMCs are ultra-asset-light (no balance sheet risk). Operating margins of 40-55% are achievable at scale. <35% suggests excessive distribution costs or poor product mix.
Sticky SIP AUM >30% of equity AUM
SIP inflows are recurring, predictable revenue. Sticky SIP book provides downside protection in bear markets when lump-sum inflows dry up.
India’s MF industry is concentrating — top 5 AMCs control ~55% of AUM. Scale = distribution leverage + brand trust + lower cost ratios.
Performance drives inflows. AMCs where >50% of schemes outperform their benchmark over 3 years attract organic net inflows; persistent underperformance leads to redemptions.
India has ~5Cr unique MF investors vs. ~18Cr demat accounts. Unique investor growth signals new-to-market customers, expanding the total addressable market beyond existing investors.
Active clients (traded in last month) drive revenue, not total demat accounts. Zerodha ~7M, Groww ~12M active. Growth rate signals market share and engagement trajectory.
Revenue per active user measures monetization. Discount brokers ₹150-300/month; full-service ₹500-1000. ARPU × active clients = total revenue; both must grow.
Platform businesses with tech leverage should achieve >35% EBIT margins at scale. ICICI Securities ~35%, Angel One ~30%. <25% means high customer acquisition cost drag.
Over-reliance on F&O revenue is risky — SEBI regulatory actions (lot size changes, margin rules) can disrupt. Diversified revenue (MF distribution, MTF, IPO) is safer.
CAC payback period signals unit economics. If CAC recovers in <6 months, each new client is value-accretive quickly. >12 months = burning cash for growth.
Platform reliability is critical during high-volume trading sessions. Outages cause client churn and regulatory penalties. In-house tech (vs. vendor) gives edge.
Asset-light broking model should generate very high ROCE. >30% is achievable at scale; <20% means either the margin is thin or capital is tied up in MTF book inefficiently.
Transaction revenue is directly linked to trading volumes. NSE’s equity ADTV >>₹1L Cr. Higher turnover = more transaction fees, clearing charges, and listing revenue.
Exchanges are natural monopolies/duopolies with massive operating leverage. BSE ~30%, NSE >60% (estimated). Marginal cost of processing one more trade is near-zero.
Network effects create winner-take-all dynamics. NSE ~93% of equity derivatives. BSE dominates SME IPOs. Market leadership in any segment is a durable moat.
Over-reliance on one product (e.g., F&O transaction charges) is regulatory risk. Diversification into data services, co-location, listing fees, mutual fund platform reduces concentration.
Exchange uptime and latency are non-negotiable. Any outage affects millions of participants and invites regulatory penalties. NSE’s co-location controversy shows reputational risk from tech issues.
Exchanges hold large settlement guarantee funds (SGF) and margin deposits. Treasury yield on this corpus (₹50,000+ Cr for NSE) is a significant income stream at near-zero risk.
Revenue grows with corporate bond issuance, bank loan ratings (RBI mandated), and surveillance fees. India’s bond market deepening drives structural 15%+ growth.
Rating agencies are opinion businesses — zero balance sheet risk, minimal capex. CRISIL ~38-42%, ICRA ~35%. >40% reflects pricing power and operating leverage at scale.
CRISIL (S&P subsidiary) and ICRA (Moody’s) dominate. Market share in rating mandates = recurring surveillance revenue. Oligopolistic structure protects margins.
Asset-light, knowledge-based business. ROCE >40% is standard for rating agencies globally (Moody’s >50%). <30% signals cost bloat or unprofitable diversification.
Default rate consistent with assigned ratings
Post-IL&FS, regulatory scrutiny on rating quality intensified. Agencies with low unexpected default rates retain credibility and mandates. One major miss = reputational damage.
Diversification into research, risk solutions, and ESG services reduces cyclical dependence on bond issuance volumes. CRISIL’s research business provides stable, recurring revenue.
AUC drives custody fees. CDSL/NSDL benefit from rising demat accounts (18Cr+ total). More accounts = more annual maintenance + transaction charges.
Settlement and clearing volumes drive per-transaction revenue. FPI custody, MF settlement, and corporate action processing all contribute. Volume is the key revenue lever.
Technology-heavy, people-light businesses with scale advantages. CDSL ~65% EBIT margin. High operating leverage — incremental revenue flows to bottom line.
Asset-light infrastructure with regulatory moat. CDSL ROCE >35%. High capital returns justify premium valuations as the business scales with India’s capital market deepening.
CDSL vs. NSDL duopoly in depository; NSE Clearing dominates clearing. Market share is sticky due to switching costs and regulatory barriers to new entrants.
Clearing corps guarantee trade settlement. Any failure is systemic risk. Track record of zero default days and robust settlement guarantee fund (SGF) is non-negotiable.
Diversified financials (Bajaj Finserv, L&T Finance) must generate consolidated ROE >15% across lending, insurance, and wealth segments to justify conglomerate structure.
Justification for diversification is cross-selling. If each customer uses ≥2 products, the conglomerate adds value vs. standalone entities. <1.5 means the subsidiaries operate in silos.
Sum-of-the-parts should exceed holding company market cap. Persistent holdco discount (>20%) means market doesn’t value the diversification — simplification may unlock value.
If one segment dominates (>50%), the “diversified” label adds complexity without benefit. True diversification = balanced contribution from lending, insurance, asset mgmt, etc.
The parent must allocate capital efficiently across subsidiaries. Every segment should earn ROIC above WACC; otherwise, capital is being subsidized from profitable segments to weak ones.
Diversified financials face multiple regulators (RBI, IRDAI, SEBI, NHB). Compliance failure in any subsidiary impacts group reputation and can trigger regulatory contagion.
Unified digital platform reduces acquisition costs and enables cross-sell. Bajaj Finserv app, Paytm, and PhonePe models show platform approach to financial services distribution.
Investment companies (holding cos like Tata Investment Corp, Bajaj Holdings) are valued on their portfolio NAV. NAV growth should at minimum match Nifty 50 to justify holding.
Market typically discounts holding companies 30-60% to NAV. <30% discount signals market trust in management; >50% means the structure destroys value vs. direct ownership.
Investment companies earn dividends from portfolio holdings. Dividend yield >2% provides tangible return while waiting for NAV discount to narrow.
Concentrated in blue-chip, liquid stocks = transparent, realizable NAV. Unlisted or illiquid holdings are hard to value and realize, widening the holdco discount.
Passive holding cos that never monetize or reallocate underperform. Active management (booking profits, reallocating to higher-return opportunities) narrows discount over time.
PAR 30+ (loans overdue >30 days) is the early warning metric for MFIs. <3% is healthy; >5% signals regional stress or over-lending. Microfinance NPAs spike fast due to group lending dynamics.
MFI lending rates are RBI-regulated (benchmark + spread cap). Yield of 18-22% covers high operating costs (doorstep collections) and credit losses while staying within regulatory caps.
MFIs have high operating costs (field officers, cash handling, group meetings). <6% opex ratio signals operational efficiency; >8% means the cost structure isn’t scalable.
State-level political risk (loan waivers, Andhra crisis 2010) can wipe out portfolios. Diversification across ≥8 states limits single-state exposure. CreditAccess, Fusion spread across 15+ states.
MFIs need high ROE to attract growth capital. 15%+ ROE is achievable with 18-22% yields, <3% credit costs, and 4-5x leverage. Below 12% signals structural unprofitability.
Repeat borrowers are lower-risk (proven repayment history) and lower-cost to acquire. >75% retention = healthy portfolio quality and customer satisfaction with loan terms.
RBI requires 15% for NBFC-MFIs. >20% provides buffer for growth and absorbs NPA shocks. Under-capitalized MFIs face lending restrictions and higher borrowing costs.

DFIs (PFC, REC, IREDA, SIDBI) are leveraged institutions lending to infrastructure/priority sectors. ROE >14% signals the spread over cost of equity is adequate. PFC/REC typically achieve 15-18% ROE due to high leverage (7-10x) on thin NIMs.

DFIs borrow at sovereign/near-sovereign rates (bonds, RBI facilities) and lend to DISCOMs, RE developers, and PSUs. NIM of 2.5-3.5% is healthy. Below 2% signals aggressive pricing to maintain market share or rising borrowing costs. PFC ~3.2%, REC ~3.0%.

DFI lending is concentrated in power/infrastructure — inherently lumpy and exposed to DISCOM financial health. GNPA <3% indicates disciplined underwriting. PFC/REC saw GNPA spike to >6% during DISCOM stress (2018-19); Govt reforms (UDAY, LPS scheme) brought it below 3%. Single large borrower default can spike GNPA significantly given concentrated loan books.

DFI loan growth tracks infrastructure capex cycle. India's ₹111 lakh Cr NIP (National Infrastructure Pipeline) and 500 GW RE target drive structural lending demand. PFC/REC targeting 12-15% loan growth. IREDA (green energy DFI) growing at 25%+ as RE financing surges.

DFIs borrow through bonds, ECBs, and institutional credit. AAA-rated DFIs (PFC, REC) borrow at 7-7.5% vs. NBFCs at 8.5-9.5%. Each 25 bps lower cost directly expands NIM. Sovereign backing and tax-free bond issuance give DFIs structural borrowing cost advantage over private lenders.

RBI mandates 15% CRAR for infrastructure finance companies. >18% provides buffer for accelerated lending growth without equity dilution. DFIs with strong capital positions can grow loan books 15-20% organically vs. capital-constrained peers requiring frequent bond/equity issuance.
No single sector >50% of loan book

PFC/REC have 90%+ exposure to power sector — extreme concentration risk. IREDA is 100% renewable energy. SIDBI is diversified across MSME segments. Diversification across power, roads, railways, telecom, RE reduces correlation of defaults. Single-sector DFIs must compensate with superior asset quality monitoring.

Financial Services (Others) includes payment processors, fintech infrastructure, financial data providers, and niche financial intermediaries. Revenue growth >15% signals product adoption and market expansion. India's financial digitization (UPI >12B monthly transactions) drives structural demand for payment rails, KYC/AML solutions, and credit infrastructure.

Technology-driven financial services (payment gateways, account aggregators, credit bureaus) are asset-light with high operating leverage.
